sentences.office: Please share your design portfolio before the interview.
sentences.interview: A diversified portfolio protects against market volatility.
sentences.business: The portfolio manager reallocated assets based on market trends.
sentences.formal: The client reviewed the portfolio before approving the campaign.
sentences.informal: My Netflix watchlist is my entertainment portfolio!

words.usageNote: Investment portfolio = all your financial assets. Professional portfolio = samples of your work. Same word, two uses.

words.simpleMeaning: a 12-month period used for financial planning and reporting

sentences.daily: India's fiscal year runs from April 1 to March 31.
sentences.office: The company reported record profits for the fiscal year.
sentences.interview: Budget planning starts six months before the fiscal year ends.
sentences.business: All financial statements are submitted at fiscal year close.
sentences.formal: The fiscal year results exceeded analyst expectations by 12%.
sentences.informal: By the end of the fiscal year, I need to file my taxes!

words.usageNote: Fiscal year ≠ calendar year. In India: April–March. In the US: often January–December.

words.simpleMeaning: the gain or loss from an investment compared to its cost

sentences.daily: Every campaign should be measured by its ROI.
sentences.office: The training program delivered a 300% ROI.
sentences.interview: Can you calculate the ROI on this marketing spend?
sentences.business: ROI = (Gain − Cost) / Cost × 100%.
sentences.formal: The project delivered a strong ROI within 6 months.
sentences.informal: My ROI on that Netflix subscription: 100% happiness!

words.usageNote: ROI = (Net Profit / Cost of Investment) × 100. Higher ROI = better use of money.

words.simpleMeaning: a measurable value showing how well something performs

sentences.daily: Our KPIs include customer satisfaction and revenue growth.
sentences.office: We track weekly KPIs to monitor team performance.
sentences.interview: What KPIs did you use to measure success in your role?
sentences.business: KPIs must be Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound.
sentences.formal: The dashboard displays all operational KPIs in real time.
sentences.informal: My personal KPI: 8 hours of sleep — rarely achieved!

words.usageNote: KPIs should be SMART. Review them regularly. Vanity metrics (likes, followers) are NOT good KPIs.

words.simpleMeaning: a list of topics to be discussed in a meeting

sentences.daily: The meeting agenda was shared 24 hours in advance.
sentences.office: Please review the agenda before tomorrow's discussion.
sentences.interview: I always prepare an agenda before any meeting.
sentences.business: The client agenda included product demo, pricing, and Q&A.
sentences.formal: The formal agenda was circulated to all attendees.
sentences.informal: No agenda? That meeting is going to be a waste!

words.usageNote: 'Agenda' = what you plan to DISCUSS. 'Minutes' = what you actually SAID. Both are meeting essentials.

words.simpleMeaning: an official written record of what was discussed in a meeting

sentences.daily: Who will take the minutes in today's meeting?
sentences.office: The meeting minutes were emailed to all participants.
sentences.interview: Review last week's minutes before we begin.
sentences.business: Minutes must accurately record all decisions made.
sentences.formal: The secretary maintained the minutes of all board meetings.
sentences.informal: I wasn't paying attention — can I see the minutes?

words.usageNote: 'Take/keep/record the minutes.' Minutes are written AFTER the meeting. Agenda is written BEFORE.

words.simpleMeaning: to assign tasks to others; or a representative

sentences.daily: Good managers know when to delegate tasks.
sentences.office: I delegated the report to my junior and reviewed it.
sentences.interview: Effective leaders delegate without losing accountability.
sentences.business: The project manager delegated research tasks to the team.
sentences.formal: Each delegate at the conference represented a different region.
sentences.informal: I delegated cooking to my sister — she over-salted it!

words.usageNote: Delegate tasks DOWN (to junior staff). Escalate issues UP (to managers). Both are important skills.

words.simpleMeaning: to work together with others

sentences.daily: I love to collaborate with creative people.
sentences.office: Our teams collaborated across three different cities.
sentences.interview: I collaborated with designers to build the app.
sentences.business: Cross-functional collaboration led to faster product delivery.
sentences.formal: The two firms collaborated on a joint sustainability initiative.
sentences.informal: I collaborated with my neighbour to complain about the noise!

words.usageNote: Collaborate WITH someone ON something: 'We collaborated with the design team on the new interface.'

words.simpleMeaning: to put a plan or decision into action

sentences.daily: We implemented the new policy across all departments.
sentences.office: It's time to implement the feedback we received.
sentences.interview: I implemented a new filing system that saved 2 hours daily.
sentences.business: The team implemented the changes within the agreed timeline.
sentences.formal: The policy was implemented after board-level approval.
sentences.informal: We implemented a 'no meetings before 10 AM' rule — paradise!

words.usageNote: Implement comes AFTER planning. You plan first, then implement. Don't skip the planning step.

words.simpleMeaning: to decide which tasks are most important and do them first

sentences.daily: I prioritize my top three tasks every morning.
sentences.office: Can you prioritize this request? It's urgent.
sentences.interview: Successful people prioritize health, family, and work — in that order.
sentences.business: We need to prioritize customer feedback over internal reports.
sentences.formal: Please prioritize the deliverables based on client urgency.
sentences.informal: I prioritize sleep — everything else can wait till morning!

words.usageNote: 'Prioritize' = decide what matters MOST right now. Use urgency + importance grid (Eisenhower Matrix).

words.simpleMeaning: a short summary of information; to give instructions

sentences.daily: I prepared a brief for the client meeting.
sentences.office: The manager briefed the team before the presentation.
sentences.interview: Keep your report brief — no more than two pages.
sentences.business: The marketing brief defined target audience and key message.
sentences.formal: Each department head was briefed before the press conference.
sentences.informal: Brief me on the weekend plans — quickly please!

words.usageNote: As a noun: a document with instructions. As a verb: to inform someone. As adjective: short and concise.

words.simpleMeaning: an action taken after a first meeting or event

sentences.daily: I sent a follow-up email after the interview.
sentences.office: Always do a follow-up within 24 hours of a meeting.
sentences.interview: The follow-up call confirmed the client's decision.
sentences.business: We scheduled a follow-up to track action item progress.
sentences.formal: The follow-up meeting was held one week after the initial discussion.
sentences.informal: I waited for their follow-up — it never came!

words.usageNote: Always follow up after interviews, meetings, and proposals. It shows professionalism and keeps you top of mind.

words.simpleMeaning: to discuss what happened after an event or project

sentences.daily: We debriefed after the product launch to learn lessons.
sentences.office: The team was debriefed after the client presentation.
sentences.interview: A post-event debrief helps improve future projects.
sentences.business: The project manager conducted a debrief with all stakeholders.
sentences.formal: After the campaign, a full debrief was held with the agency.
sentences.informal: Full debrief after my exam: I should have studied more!

words.usageNote: Debrief = look BACKWARD (after an event). Brief = look FORWARD (before an event). Both are essential.

words.simpleMeaning: to refer a problem to a higher authority

sentences.daily: If you can't solve it, escalate to your manager.
sentences.office: The complaint was escalated to the regional director.
sentences.interview: Don't wait — escalate the issue before the deadline.
sentences.business: The bug was escalated to the engineering lead for a fix.
sentences.formal: The unresolved dispute was escalated to the legal team.
sentences.informal: I escalated the slow internet complaint to three people — still slow!

words.usageNote: Escalate = take the issue to someone with MORE authority. Use when you've tried but can't resolve it.

words.simpleMeaning: to make a process simpler and more efficient

sentences.daily: We streamlined the approval process from 5 steps to 2.
sentences.office: Technology can streamline repetitive office tasks.
sentences.interview: My goal is to streamline our reporting workflow.
sentences.business: The new software streamlined order management significantly.
sentences.formal: The company streamlined operations to reduce costs by 20%.
sentences.informal: I streamlined my morning routine — now I'm late in just 15 minutes!

words.usageNote: Streamline = remove steps that don't add value. Always ask: 'Can this process be simpler?'

words.simpleMeaning: the time to complete a task, or a major improvement

sentences.daily: The turnaround time for visa approval is 5 days.
sentences.office: What is your turnaround for this design project?
sentences.interview: The company achieved a remarkable financial turnaround.
sentences.business: Fast turnaround is essential in client-facing roles.
sentences.formal: A 24-hour turnaround was agreed upon with the client.
sentences.informal: My turnaround on bad moods is a cup of chai and sleep!

words.usageNote: Two meanings: 1) How fast you complete something. 2) Reversing a declining business/situation.

words.simpleMeaning: to send or distribute something to many people

sentences.daily: Please circulate the agenda before the meeting.
sentences.office: The report was circulated to all department heads.
sentences.interview: I circulated the survey link to 500 employees.
sentences.business: The memo was circulated internally for comment.
sentences.formal: The draft proposal was circulated for review and feedback.
sentences.informal: That embarrassing video circulated the whole office instantly!

words.usageNote: 'Circulate a document/report/memo' = send it to all relevant people. Very common in office communication.

words.simpleMeaning: to officially approve or support something

sentences.daily: The manager endorsed the project proposal.
sentences.office: I would endorse this candidate wholeheartedly.
sentences.interview: The board endorsed the new sustainability policy.
sentences.business: Three senior leaders endorsed the initiative publicly.
sentences.formal: The plan was endorsed by the committee after revisions.
sentences.informal: She endorsed my samosa business — two thumbs up!

words.usageNote: Endorse = public or official support. LinkedIn endorsements = colleagues confirming your skills.

words.simpleMeaning: a person who acts as a link between groups

sentences.daily: She acts as liaison between the client and our team.
sentences.office: Good liaison between departments prevents miscommunication.
sentences.interview: I serve as the liaison for international operations.
sentences.business: The project liaison ensured smooth communication.
sentences.formal: The regional liaison reported to headquarters weekly.
sentences.informal: My mom is my family liaison — she tells everyone my business!

words.usageNote: 'Liaison officer' = a person assigned to communicate between two groups. 'Act as liaison' is very common.

words.simpleMeaning: general agreement among a group

sentences.daily: We reached a consensus after a two-hour discussion.
sentences.office: Building consensus takes time but produces better results.
sentences.interview: The team achieved consensus on the project timeline.
sentences.business: The policy was adopted after cross-functional consensus.
sentences.formal: A consensus decision means everyone agrees — or at least accepts.
sentences.informal: Consensus on dinner: biryani. Final answer.

words.usageNote: 'Reach/build/achieve consensus.' Consensus ≠ unanimous. It means most agree or nobody strongly objects.

words.simpleMeaning: to recognize or confirm you have received or understood something

sentences.daily: Please acknowledge receipt of this email.
sentences.office: The manager acknowledged the team's hard work.
sentences.interview: I acknowledge that I made an error in the report.
sentences.business: Always acknowledge emails within 24 hours.
sentences.formal: The CEO acknowledged the staff's contribution at the event.
sentences.informal: I acknowledged the problem — fixing it is another story!

words.usageNote: 'Acknowledge receipt' = confirm you got it. Very important in professional email communication.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something clearer or easier to understand

sentences.daily: Please clarify the deadline — is it Friday or Monday?
sentences.office: I asked the client to clarify their requirements.
sentences.interview: Can you clarify what you mean by 'urgent'?
sentences.business: The project scope was clarified in writing to avoid confusion.
sentences.formal: The HR team clarified the leave policy for all employees.
sentences.informal: Can you clarify? I zoned out for the last 10 minutes.

words.usageNote: 'Clarify' vs 'Confirm': clarify = make unclear things clear. Confirm = verify something already said.

words.simpleMeaning: to delay an event to a later time

sentences.daily: The meeting was postponed due to the manager's travel.
sentences.office: I had to postpone my vacation for a project deadline.
sentences.interview: Can we postpone the call to Thursday?
sentences.business: The product launch was postponed after testing issues.
sentences.formal: The event was postponed to the following quarter.
sentences.informal: I keep postponing my gym start date — next Monday again!

words.usageNote: 'Postpone' = push to a later time. 'Cancel' = not happening at all. Very different consequences!

words.simpleMeaning: referring a problem to a higher level of authority

sentences.daily: This complaint requires immediate escalation to the VP.
sentences.office: Escalation should be the last resort, not the first.
sentences.interview: The escalation matrix defines who handles what issue.
sentences.business: An escalation was triggered after the client complained twice.
sentences.formal: All escalations were logged and tracked in the CRM.
sentences.informal: Escalation: when you complain to the manager and the manager complains to their manager!

words.usageNote: Escalation path: Team Member → Manager → Director → VP → CEO. Use the right level!

words.simpleMeaning: official written record of what was decided in a meeting

sentences.daily: The meeting minutes were shared within 30 minutes.
sentences.office: Could you take the minutes today?
sentences.interview: Refer to last week's minutes for the action items.
sentences.business: Minutes should capture decisions, owners, and deadlines.
sentences.formal: The company secretary maintained minutes for all board meetings.
sentences.informal: I missed the meeting — at least I can read the minutes!

words.usageNote: 'Take minutes' = write them. 'Read/review minutes' = look at past records. Not the same as 'agenda'.

words.simpleMeaning: a specific task someone must complete after a meeting

sentences.daily: Each action item must have an owner and a deadline.
sentences.office: Review the action items from last week's meeting.
sentences.interview: My action item: send the report by 5 PM Friday.
sentences.business: Action items were tracked in the shared project board.
sentences.formal: All action items from the sprint review were assigned.
sentences.informal: Action item from family meeting: someone clean the fridge — everyone is looking at me!

words.usageNote: Action items must have 3 parts: WHAT (task), WHO (owner), WHEN (deadline). Without all 3, things get lost.

words.simpleMeaning: the sequence of steps to complete a process

sentences.daily: Our content workflow goes from draft to review to publish.
sentences.office: I mapped out the approval workflow step by step.
sentences.interview: Automating repetitive workflow steps saves hours weekly.
sentences.business: The development workflow followed an agile sprint cycle.
sentences.formal: The approval workflow was redesigned to reduce bottlenecks.
sentences.informal: My morning workflow: alarm, snooze, panic, rush!

words.usageNote: Workflow = how work MOVES through a system. Optimise your workflow to eliminate bottlenecks and delays.

words.simpleMeaning: time taken to complete and return a piece of work

sentences.daily: Our turnaround time for proposals is 48 hours.
sentences.office: Fast turnaround time is a key competitive advantage.
sentences.interview: What is your turnaround time for design revisions?
sentences.business: The client demanded a 24-hour turnaround on the contract.
sentences.formal: Turnaround time was reduced from 5 days to 2 by automation.
sentences.informal: Turnaround time for deciding where to eat: 45 minutes!

words.usageNote: Turnaround time = from when you receive work to when you deliver it back. Keep it short and consistent.

words.simpleMeaning: how much work a person or system can handle

sentences.daily: I don't have the bandwidth to take on new projects.
sentences.office: Do you have bandwidth this week to help with the event?
sentences.interview: Check team bandwidth before assigning more tasks.
sentences.business: The server's bandwidth was upgraded to handle peak traffic.
sentences.formal: She doesn't have the bandwidth — her calendar is full.
sentences.informal: No bandwidth for drama — I'm fully booked this week!

words.usageNote: In tech: bandwidth = data speed. In work: bandwidth = how much capacity (time/energy) you have available.

words.simpleMeaning: a point in a process where things slow down

sentences.daily: Approval delays are the bottleneck in our process.
sentences.office: Identify the bottleneck before trying to speed things up.
sentences.interview: Data entry is the biggest bottleneck in our workflow.
sentences.business: The single-point review created a major process bottleneck.
sentences.formal: Removing the bottleneck reduced delivery time by 30%.
sentences.informal: Traffic jam at the signal — classic bottleneck!

words.usageNote: Bottleneck = one slow point that slows EVERYTHING else. Fix the bottleneck first; the rest flows faster.

words.simpleMeaning: a specific output or result that must be produced

sentences.daily: The main deliverable is a 10-page strategy report.
sentences.office: What are the key deliverables for this project?
sentences.interview: Each deliverable has a deadline and an owner.
sentences.business: All deliverables were submitted on time and on budget.
sentences.formal: The deliverables were reviewed and approved by the client.
sentences.informal: My key deliverable this weekend: doing laundry. Delayed again!

words.usageNote: Every project must have clear deliverables. 'What will you produce?' = deliverable.

words.simpleMeaning: the process of keeping all involved parties happy and informed

sentences.daily: Effective stakeholder management ensures project success.
sentences.office: Stakeholder management requires clear, regular communication.
sentences.interview: I managed 15 stakeholders across 3 departments.
sentences.business: Stakeholder management is a critical skill for project managers.
sentences.formal: The PM's stakeholder management prevented a major conflict.
sentences.informal: Stakeholder management at home: keeping parents and friends both happy!

words.usageNote: Key: identify stakeholders early, understand their interests, and communicate proactively. No surprises!

words.simpleMeaning: Key Performance Indicators — measurable goals

sentences.daily: We review our KPIs every Monday morning.
sentences.office: What KPIs are you tracking for customer satisfaction?
sentences.interview: Setting clear KPIs helps the team focus on results.
sentences.business: The dashboard displays all team KPIs in real time.
sentences.formal: KPIs were revised at the start of each new quarter.
sentences.informal: My personal KPI: inbox zero by 6 PM — always fails!

words.usageNote: KPIs must be measurable and time-bound. 'Improve customer service' is NOT a KPI. 'Respond in <2 hrs' IS one.

words.simpleMeaning: a step-by-step instruction for a standard process

sentences.daily: Follow the SOP for onboarding new employees.
sentences.office: Every department should have SOPs for recurring tasks.
sentences.interview: We created an SOP for social media posting.
sentences.business: The SOP ensures consistent quality across all branches.
sentences.formal: SOPs were reviewed and updated at the start of each year.
sentences.informal: Even morning chai has an SOP in this office!

words.usageNote: SOP = a documented, repeatable process. SOPs reduce errors, save training time, and ensure consistency.

words.simpleMeaning: to update or align information; an informal meeting

sentences.daily: Let's sync on the project status this afternoon.
sentences.office: Are you synced with the latest version of the file?
sentences.interview: I need to sync with the design team before the deadline.
sentences.business: Daily syncs help the team stay aligned on priorities.
sentences.formal: A quick sync call was scheduled for Friday morning.
sentences.informal: My Bluetooth and brain are both out of sync today!

words.usageNote: 'Quick sync' = short meeting to update each other. 'Sync up' = make sure everyone has the same information.

words.simpleMeaning: to change direction or strategy

sentences.daily: The startup pivoted from B2C to B2B after poor sales.
sentences.office: We may need to pivot our approach based on feedback.
sentences.interview: When one strategy fails, pivot — don't give up.
sentences.business: The team pivoted to a new product segment in Q3.
sentences.formal: A strategic pivot was announced at the all-hands meeting.
sentences.informal: Instagram pivoted from a whiskey app to a photo app — true story!

words.usageNote: 'Pivot' = change direction while keeping core strengths. Not giving up — it's adapting strategically.

words.simpleMeaning: capacity to take on more work

sentences.daily: Sorry, I don't have bandwidth for this right now.
sentences.office: Let's check team bandwidth before adding tasks.
sentences.interview: The team's bandwidth is fully used this sprint.
sentences.business: I'll have more bandwidth after Tuesday's deadline.
sentences.formal: Bandwidth is tight with three parallel projects running.
sentences.informal: Zero bandwidth — my brain is full!

words.usageNote: In modern offices, 'bandwidth' is commonly used to mean AVAILABLE TIME AND ENERGY, not internet speed.

words.simpleMeaning: to include someone in a conversation or email chain

sentences.daily: Loop in the finance team before sending the proposal.
sentences.office: Please loop me in on any client communication.
sentences.interview: I looped in the HR manager for the policy question.
sentences.business: Always loop in stakeholders before making major decisions.
sentences.formal: The regional director was looped in via email.
sentences.informal: Loop me in on the lunch plans — I'm coming!

words.usageNote: 'Loop in' or 'loop someone in' = make sure they're included and informed. 'Please loop in Priya.'

words.simpleMeaning: to finish or conclude something

sentences.daily: Let's wrap up this meeting in 5 minutes.
sentences.office: I need 30 minutes to wrap up this report.
sentences.interview: We wrapped up the project ahead of schedule.
sentences.business: Please wrap up your presentation by 3 PM.
sentences.formal: The conference was wrapped up with a keynote address.
sentences.informal: Wrap up the argument — I'm hungry and need dinner!

words.usageNote: 'Wrap up' = end something neatly and completely. 'Let's wrap up' is one of the most-used office phrases.

words.simpleMeaning: to make contact with someone

sentences.daily: Please reach out to the client by tomorrow.
sentences.office: I reached out to three vendors for quotes.
sentences.interview: Feel free to reach out if you have any questions.
sentences.business: Reach out to HR if you face any workplace issues.
sentences.formal: The team lead reached out to the consultant for advice.
sentences.informal: I reached out to my old friend — turns out they're married now!

words.usageNote: 'Reach out to someone' = make first contact. Very common in professional emails and LinkedIn messages.

words.simpleMeaning: to briefly communicate with someone to stay updated

sentences.daily: I'll touch base with you after the client call.
sentences.office: Can we touch base tomorrow morning to align?
sentences.interview: Let's touch base weekly to track progress.
sentences.business: The manager touched base with each team member briefly.
sentences.formal: The director touched base with branch heads every Monday.
sentences.informal: Touching base: 5-minute chat or 3-hour meeting? You decide!

words.usageNote: 'Touch base' = a quick, brief interaction to stay in sync. Usually informal. 'Just touching base!'

words.simpleMeaning: to give responsibility or a task to someone else

sentences.daily: I'll hand off the project to Priya on Friday.
sentences.office: Make sure all details are ready before the hand-off.
sentences.interview: The client was handed off from sales to customer success.
sentences.business: A smooth hand-off prevents information loss.
sentences.formal: The outgoing team member handed off all documentation.
sentences.informal: Hand-off the TV remote only when the cricket match ends!

words.usageNote: 'Hand off' = transfer ownership of a task or responsibility. Document everything before handing off.

words.simpleMeaning: to give final approval to something

sentences.daily: Can you sign off on this invoice today?
sentences.office: The project needs the director's sign-off before launch.
sentences.interview: I signed off on the final budget last evening.
sentences.business: All changes require manager sign-off before implementation.
sentences.formal: The client signed off on the design after two rounds.
sentences.informal: Signed off on the weekend plan — now the weather disagrees!

words.usageNote: 'Sign off on something' = give your formal approval. After sign-off, no more changes without re-approval.

words.simpleMeaning: to be in charge of directing something

sentences.daily: I'd like to take the lead on this project.
sentences.office: She took the lead in organizing the team event.
sentences.interview: Taking the lead shows confidence and initiative.
sentences.business: He took the lead during the client crisis and resolved it.
sentences.formal: The senior analyst took the lead on the market study.
sentences.informal: No one took the lead at dinner so we ordered everything!

words.usageNote: 'Take the lead' = volunteer or be assigned to lead. Shows initiative. Strong interview phrase: 'I took the lead on...'

words.simpleMeaning: to formally finish and approve something

sentences.daily: Please sign off on these timesheets by end of day.
sentences.office: The manager signed off on the campaign creative.
sentences.interview: We can't go live until the legal team signs off.
sentences.business: Final sign-off requires C-suite authorization.
sentences.formal: The client signed off on the final deliverable yesterday.
sentences.informal: Boss signed off — we're going home early!

words.usageNote: 'Sign off' = final approval that closes a task or project. Nothing proceeds until sign-off.

words.simpleMeaning: to have the same understanding or agreement

sentences.daily: Let's confirm we're all on the same page before we proceed.
sentences.office: Are you and the client on the same page about scope?
sentences.interview: I called a quick meeting to make sure we're on the same page.
sentences.business: After the debrief, everyone was finally on the same page.
sentences.formal: The briefing ensured all stakeholders were on the same page.
sentences.informal: We're not on the same page about whose turn it is to wash dishes!

words.usageNote: 'Get/be on the same page' = ensure everyone understands the same thing. A very common office idiom.

words.simpleMeaning: to fully accept responsibility for something

sentences.daily: I take ownership of my mistakes and fix them.
sentences.office: Taking ownership of tasks builds trust with your manager.
sentences.interview: He took ownership of the failed launch and made changes.
sentences.business: Team members who take ownership are promoted faster.
sentences.formal: The lead developer took ownership of the critical bug fix.
sentences.informal: I take ownership of my terrible sense of direction!

words.usageNote: 'Take ownership' = don't make excuses, don't blame others — just do the work and own the result.

words.simpleMeaning: from now on; going ahead with a plan

sentences.daily: Moving forward, all requests must be submitted in writing.
sentences.office: Let's focus on solutions, moving forward.
sentences.interview: Moving forward, weekly check-ins will be mandatory.
sentences.business: Moving forward, the team will follow a new approval process.
sentences.formal: The revised policy takes effect moving forward from April 1.
sentences.informal: Moving forward, I will wake up at 6 AM. (Said every Sunday)

words.usageNote: 'Moving forward' = from this point on. Common in emails and meetings. Signals a shift in policy or approach.

words.simpleMeaning: availability of time and mental energy for tasks

sentences.daily: My bandwidth this week is very limited.
sentences.office: Before assigning work, check the person's bandwidth.
sentences.interview: The team is at full bandwidth with existing projects.
sentences.business: With limited bandwidth, we must prioritize ruthlessly.
sentences.formal: The manager assessed team bandwidth before adding deliverables.
sentences.informal: Zero bandwidth — even breathing is scheduled!

words.usageNote: A person's bandwidth is not infinite. Respecting it = respecting them. Overloading leads to burnout and errors.

words.simpleMeaning: taking action without being told to

sentences.daily: She took the initiative to redesign the client report.
sentences.office: I appreciate employees who show initiative.
sentences.interview: Taking initiative shows you are a self-starter.
sentences.business: The initiative to automate billing saved 10 hours weekly.
sentences.formal: The employee's initiative impressed the senior leadership team.
sentences.informal: No one took initiative to refill the water cooler. Surprise!

words.usageNote: 'Take the initiative' = act first without waiting to be told. A highly valued workplace quality.

words.hindi: तालमेल बिठाना / एक दिशा में करना
words.simpleMeaning: to bring things or people into agreement or arrangement

sentences.daily: We need to align our goals with the company's vision.
sentences.office: Please align your work schedule with the team's deadlines.
sentences.interview: The campaign message was aligned with brand guidelines.
sentences.business: All departments must align before the strategy is executed.
sentences.formal: The regional and global teams were fully aligned on priorities.
sentences.informal: My alarm and my body are not aligned — never will be!

words.usageNote: 'Align with' = match or agree with. 'Misalign' = when two things don't match. Both are common in management.

words.simpleMeaning: to carefully read and correct errors in a document

sentences.daily: Always proofread before sending a professional email.
sentences.office: I proofreaded the report three times before submission.
sentences.interview: Proofreading takes 10 minutes and saves hours of embarrassment.
sentences.business: The editor proofread the annual report for accuracy.
sentences.formal: All marketing materials must be proofread before publishing.
sentences.informal: I sent the email without proofreading — there were five mistakes!

words.usageNote: Proofread = read specifically to FIND ERRORS. Different from editing (improving content). Do both!

words.simpleMeaning: information about how well something has been done

sentences.daily: I always welcome constructive feedback on my work.
sentences.office: The manager gave feedback after the presentation.
sentences.interview: Feedback should be specific, timely, and actionable.
sentences.business: Customer feedback helped us improve the product significantly.
sentences.formal: 360-degree feedback was collected from all team members.
sentences.informal: Feedback received: 'Your cooking needs improvement.' Feedback ignored.

words.usageNote: 'Give/receive/seek feedback.' 'Constructive feedback' = helpful criticism. 'Positive feedback' = praise.

words.simpleMeaning: carbon copy / blind carbon copy in email

sentences.daily: CC me on all client communications.
sentences.office: I BCC'd my manager on the vendor complaint.
sentences.interview: CC = the person can see they were copied. BCC = they are hidden.
sentences.business: Always CC your supervisor when escalating to a client.
sentences.formal: The email was sent to 10 recipients and CC'd to the director.
sentences.informal: She BCC'd HR on the passive-aggressive team email. Smart move.

words.usageNote: CC = recipients see everyone included. BCC = copied but invisible to others. Use BCC for privacy.

words.simpleMeaning: the ability to do something successfully

sentences.daily: Technical competence alone is not enough in modern jobs.
sentences.office: She demonstrated clear competence in data analysis.
sentences.interview: I built my competence in Python over six months.
sentences.business: The company values competence and cultural fit equally.
sentences.formal: Core competencies were assessed in the structured interview.
sentences.informal: My competence in cooking: limited to maggi and chai.

words.usageNote: 'Core competency' = a key strength your company or role is built around. Map your skills to job requirements.

words.simpleMeaning: relating to relationships between people

sentences.daily: Strong interpersonal skills are essential in any team.
sentences.office: My interpersonal skills helped build client relationships.
sentences.interview: Interpersonal communication includes listening, empathy, and feedback.
sentences.business: The role requires excellent interpersonal skills.
sentences.formal: Interpersonal conflicts were resolved through structured mediation.
sentences.informal: My interpersonal skills failed me at the networking event — I stood in a corner!

words.usageNote: 'Interpersonal skills' = how well you work WITH other people. Key for interviews, leadership, and client roles.

words.simpleMeaning: acting without being asked; a new plan or programme

sentences.daily: I launched a peer mentoring initiative at my last job.
sentences.office: Always take initiative — don't wait to be told.
sentences.interview: Tell me about an initiative you led successfully.
sentences.business: The employee-led wellness initiative improved team morale.
sentences.formal: The company launched a digital literacy initiative for staff.
sentences.informal: Initiative: setting 10 alarms and still waking up at 9.

words.usageNote: Interviewers love: 'I took the initiative to...' Use it with a specific example and quantified result.

words.simpleMeaning: being responsible for your actions and their results

sentences.daily: Accountability means owning both successes and failures.
sentences.office: I value a culture of accountability in the workplace.
sentences.interview: Tell me about a time you demonstrated accountability.
sentences.business: High accountability leads to faster, higher-quality results.
sentences.formal: The team's accountability culture was cited as a key strength.
sentences.informal: Accountability starts with: 'I made a mistake. Here's my fix.'

words.usageNote: Accountability = doing what you said you'd do AND owning the result when it doesn't go as planned.

words.simpleMeaning: able to recover quickly from difficulties

sentences.daily: A resilient team bounces back from setbacks faster.
sentences.office: I stay resilient under pressure and meet deadlines.
sentences.interview: Tell me about a time you had to be resilient.
sentences.business: Resilient leaders inspire confidence during uncertain times.
sentences.formal: The company remained resilient despite economic challenges.
sentences.informal: I am resilient — I can watch a sad movie without crying. Sometimes.

words.usageNote: Resilience = the ability to RECOVER, not the inability to struggle. Struggling + recovering = resilient.

words.simpleMeaning: able to adjust to new conditions or changes

sentences.daily: I am adaptable and thrive in fast-changing environments.
sentences.office: Adaptable employees are invaluable during company transitions.
sentences.interview: My adaptable nature helped me succeed in three different roles.
sentences.business: The most successful companies build adaptable teams.
sentences.formal: Adaptable professionals embrace change as an opportunity.
sentences.informal: I'm adaptable — I can sleep anywhere, anytime!

words.usageNote: Adaptability = willingness and ability to change. In interviews: 'I adapted when...' with a specific story.

words.simpleMeaning: taking action before problems arise, not waiting to react

sentences.daily: I proactively flagged the issue before it became a crisis.
sentences.office: Proactive employees identify risks before they escalate.
sentences.interview: I take a proactive approach to project management.
sentences.business: Proactive customer service prevents complaints from escalating.
sentences.formal: The proactive team prepared contingency plans in advance.
sentences.informal: Proactive: charging my phone before it hits 5%. Revolutionary!

words.usageNote: Proactive = acting BEFORE problems happen. Reactive = responding AFTER. Employers want proactive people.

words.simpleMeaning: to express something as a number or quantity

sentences.daily: Quantify your achievements — say '20%' not 'many'.
sentences.office: I quantified the impact of my work using data.
sentences.interview: Can you quantify the savings your idea generated?
sentences.business: HR prefers quantified achievements over vague descriptions.
sentences.formal: The analyst quantified the cost of the system failure.
sentences.informal: How do I quantify how much I love chai? Impossible.

words.usageNote: In resumes and interviews: quantify everything. 'Improved sales' is weak. 'Grew sales by 35% in 6 months' wins.

words.simpleMeaning: to express ideas clearly and effectively

sentences.daily: She is very articulate and presents ideas brilliantly.
sentences.office: I can articulate complex ideas in simple terms.
sentences.interview: Articulate professionals advance faster in their careers.
sentences.business: The CEO was articulate in explaining the company's new vision.
sentences.formal: His articulate response impressed the interview panel.
sentences.informal: I sounded very articulate in my head — not so much out loud!

words.usageNote: 'Articulate' as adjective = speaks clearly. As a verb: 'articulate your thoughts' = express them clearly.

words.simpleMeaning: the period of time someone holds a position

sentences.daily: During my tenure at XYZ, I led a team of 12.
sentences.office: She had a successful 5-year tenure as marketing head.
sentences.interview: Tenure shows loyalty, but job-hopping shows ambition.
sentences.business: His tenure was marked by record revenue growth.
sentences.formal: Academic tenure provides job security for professors.
sentences.informal: My tenure at my last job: 2 years, 3 months, 14 days — but who's counting?

words.usageNote: 'During my tenure as...' is excellent interview language. Shows you can describe your time in a role professionally.

words.simpleMeaning: able to examine things carefully and logically

sentences.daily: I am highly analytical and love solving data problems.
sentences.office: Analytical skills are essential in finance and consulting.
sentences.interview: My analytical approach helped identify a ₹5 lakh cost saving.
sentences.business: The analyst's analytical mindset prevented a major error.
sentences.formal: Analytical competency was tested through a case study exercise.
sentences.informal: My most analytical decision: spending 30 minutes choosing a movie.

words.usageNote: 'Analytical skills' = ability to break a problem into parts and think logically. Crucial in data-heavy roles.

words.simpleMeaning: to do several tasks at the same time

sentences.daily: I can multitask effectively without missing deadlines.
sentences.office: Avoid multitasking — it often reduces quality.
sentences.interview: She multitasks effortlessly between client calls and reports.
sentences.business: Multitasking is a myth for complex, creative work.
sentences.formal: The role requires the ability to multitask in a fast-paced environment.
sentences.informal: I multitask: eating breakfast while sending emails — usually both poorly!

words.usageNote: Research shows multitasking reduces quality. Better: 'I manage multiple priorities' instead of 'I multitask.'

words.simpleMeaning: the positive qualities or skills someone has

sentences.daily: My strengths include communication and problem-solving.
sentences.office: Tell me about your strengths and how you use them.
sentences.interview: Play to your strengths in every role.
sentences.business: Knowing your strengths helps you choose the right career.
sentences.formal: Strengths were assessed through a self-evaluation exercise.
sentences.informal: My strength: I always say yes. My weakness: also that I always say yes.

words.usageNote: In interviews: mention strengths RELEVANT to the job. Back each one with a specific example. Be concrete.

words.simpleMeaning: a quality or skill that needs improvement

sentences.daily: My weakness is that I overcommit and take on too much.
sentences.office: Turn a weakness into a growth story in interviews.
sentences.interview: Always show how you're improving your weakness.
sentences.business: A common interview weakness: being too detail-oriented.
sentences.formal: Weaknesses were discussed honestly in the 360-degree review.
sentences.informal: Weakness: chocolate. I have no defence against it.

words.usageNote: Interview tip: never say 'I have no weaknesses.' Pick a REAL one and show how you're working on it.

words.simpleMeaning: something you have done successfully

sentences.daily: List your top three professional accomplishments.
sentences.office: My biggest accomplishment was reducing churn by 40%.
sentences.interview: Accomplishments speak louder than job titles.
sentences.business: She shared her accomplishments confidently and clearly.
sentences.formal: Each accomplishment was quantified on the resume.
sentences.informal: Personal accomplishment: finishing a book in one sitting — only happened once.

words.usageNote: 'Accomplishment' = something you FINISHED successfully. In interviews, use the STAR method: Situation, Task, Action, Result.

words.simpleMeaning: what you add to a team, project, or goal

sentences.daily: My key contribution was implementing the new billing system.
sentences.office: Tell me about your most significant contribution at work.
sentences.interview: I made meaningful contributions to all three projects.
sentences.business: Her contribution to the team's growth was invaluable.
sentences.formal: Each team member's contribution was recognised at the awards.
sentences.informal: My biggest contribution to family dinners: eating everything!

words.usageNote: In interviews: 'What will you contribute?' Frame your answer around the EMPLOYER'S NEEDS, not yours.

words.simpleMeaning: something that provides extra benefit beyond what's expected

sentences.daily: Strong communication skills are a value add in any role.
sentences.office: I bring value add through my bilingual proficiency.
sentences.interview: What value add can you bring to our team?
sentences.business: The free training was a significant value add for employees.
sentences.formal: Every hire should be a clear value add to the organisation.
sentences.informal: Free chai during meetings is a major value add for me!

words.usageNote: 'Value add' = something EXTRA that makes you or your work more valuable. Always highlight yours.

words.simpleMeaning: time you must work before leaving a job

sentences.daily: My notice period is 60 days as per my contract.
sentences.office: Can you negotiate a shorter notice period?
sentences.interview: Serving the full notice period is professional and respectful.
sentences.business: The candidate was available after completing the notice period.
sentences.formal: Notice periods vary from 1 month to 3 months in India.
sentences.informal: Notice period: the longest 30 days of your professional life!

words.usageNote: Typical India notice period: 1–3 months. Negotiate a buyout if you need to join faster.

words.simpleMeaning: a formal review of an employee's performance

sentences.daily: My annual appraisal is scheduled for next month.
sentences.office: Prepare for your appraisal with data, not just effort.
sentences.interview: I received an outstanding rating in my appraisal.
sentences.business: The appraisal process includes self-review and manager review.
sentences.formal: Post-appraisal increments were announced in April.
sentences.informal: Pre-appraisal mood: nervous. Post-appraisal mood: depends.

words.usageNote: 'Performance appraisal' = formal annual or half-yearly review that determines pay raises and promotions.

words.simpleMeaning: total yearly cost a company pays for an employee

sentences.daily: My current CTC is ₹8 LPA — I'm looking for ₹12 LPA.
sentences.office: CTC includes salary, PF, gratuity, and all benefits.
sentences.interview: Always clarify the in-hand amount separately from CTC.
sentences.business: The offer letter listed CTC of ₹10 lakh per annum.
sentences.formal: CTC often looks larger than the actual take-home salary.
sentences.informal: CTC ₹10 LPA sounds great until you see the take-home!

words.usageNote: CTC ≠ In-hand. CTC includes employer PF, health insurance, bonuses. In-hand = what hits your bank account.

words.simpleMeaning: the main areas of work a person is responsible for

sentences.daily: My KRAs are sales targets, client retention, and team leadership.
sentences.office: Set clear KRAs at the start of each quarter.
sentences.interview: My manager and I defined my KRAs in the first week.
sentences.business: KRAs determine what you're evaluated on during appraisal.
sentences.formal: Employees who exceed their KRAs are considered for fast-track promotions.
sentences.informal: KRA: don't be late. Status: always late. Result: appraisal awkward.

words.usageNote: KRA = what you are primarily ACCOUNTABLE for. KPI = how you MEASURE success in those areas. Both matter.

words.simpleMeaning: a meeting when an employee is leaving to gather feedback

sentences.daily: The HR team conducted an exit interview before I left.
sentences.office: Be honest but diplomatic in your exit interview.
sentences.interview: Exit interviews help companies understand why employees leave.
sentences.business: She used the exit interview to give constructive feedback.
sentences.formal: Exit interview insights were compiled in an annual retention report.
sentences.informal: Exit interview question: 'Why are you leaving?' Answer: 'Netflix was calling.'

words.usageNote: Be professional in exit interviews. Don't burn bridges — industries are smaller than you think.

words.simpleMeaning: the process of introducing a new employee to the company

sentences.daily: The onboarding process took 2 weeks at my last company.
sentences.office: Good onboarding reduces employee turnover significantly.
sentences.interview: I redesigned the onboarding program for new hires.
sentences.business: Smooth onboarding sets employees up for success.
sentences.formal: The onboarding experience was rated 4.8/5 by new joiners.
sentences.informal: Onboarding at my first job: here's your laptop, here's your tea, figure it out!

words.usageNote: Effective onboarding = role clarity + team introductions + tools access + culture immersion. All four matter.

words.simpleMeaning: changing to a job at the same level but in a different area

sentences.daily: I made a lateral move from sales to marketing.
sentences.office: Lateral moves build diverse skills and broaden your career.
sentences.interview: Not all growth is upward — lateral moves matter.
sentences.business: The company encouraged lateral moves to develop future leaders.
sentences.formal: Her lateral move from operations to strategy proved transformative.
sentences.informal: Promotion denied? Make a lateral move — gather skills instead!

words.usageNote: Lateral move = same level, different function. Builds breadth. Useful when a vertical promotion isn't available.

words.simpleMeaning: to learn new skills to improve your abilities

sentences.daily: I upskilled in Python during the lockdown.
sentences.office: Companies invest in upskilling to retain top talent.
sentences.interview: You must continuously upskill to stay relevant.
sentences.business: The training programme was designed to upskill the sales team.
sentences.formal: Employees who upskill regularly are promoted more frequently.
sentences.informal: Upskilled in Excel — now I'm the office hero at pivot tables!

words.usageNote: 'Upskill' = learn NEW skills for your existing or future role. Reskill = learn different skills for a new role.

words.simpleMeaning: non-technical interpersonal skills

sentences.daily: Soft skills like communication and empathy are crucial.
sentences.office: Employers value soft skills as much as technical ability.
sentences.interview: I strengthened my soft skills through team leadership.
sentences.business: Soft skills are harder to teach than hard skills.
sentences.formal: The assessment included both technical and soft skill evaluation.
sentences.informal: I have excellent soft skills — I'm great at apologizing!

words.usageNote: Soft skills: communication, empathy, teamwork, leadership. Hard skills: Excel, coding, accounting. BOTH are essential.

words.simpleMeaning: specific, teachable technical abilities

sentences.daily: My hard skills include Python, SQL, and data visualisation.
sentences.office: List your hard skills with certification names.
sentences.interview: Hard skills get you the interview; soft skills get you the job.
sentences.business: The role requires hard skills in project management tools.
sentences.formal: Hard skills were verified through a technical assessment.
sentences.informal: Hard skill: parallel parking. Mastery level: never achieved.

words.usageNote: Hard skills can be learned and measured. Example: 'certified in Salesforce CRM' is a measurable hard skill.

words.simpleMeaning: a year taken off between studies or work to travel or grow

sentences.daily: I took a gap year to travel and find my purpose.
sentences.office: A gap year isn't a weakness — it's a story.
sentences.interview: Explain your gap year positively in interviews.
sentences.business: She used her gap year to complete two certifications.
sentences.formal: A gap year between jobs is becoming more accepted globally.
sentences.informal: Gap year plan: travel the world. Actual gap year: sat at home in pyjamas.

words.usageNote: Never apologise for a gap year. Frame it: 'I used that time to...' Focus on what you LEARNED or GAINED.

words.simpleMeaning: people who can vouch for your work and character

sentences.daily: Please provide two professional references.
sentences.office: Always inform your references before sharing their details.
sentences.interview: Strong references can make a big difference in hiring.
sentences.business: Her references praised her leadership and attention to detail.
sentences.formal: References were contacted before the final offer was made.
sentences.informal: Reference check: 'Is she punctual?' Reference sweating nervously.

words.usageNote: Always ask permission before listing someone as a reference. Choose people who know your work, not just friends.

words.simpleMeaning: someone who works independently without being told what to do

sentences.daily: I am a self-starter — I identify and solve problems independently.
sentences.office: Startups need self-starters who don't need micromanagement.
sentences.interview: Being a self-starter helped me build a side business.
sentences.business: The role is ideal for a self-starter comfortable with ambiguity.
sentences.formal: Self-starters require minimal supervision and deliver results.
sentences.informal: Self-starter: set 5 goals today. Starter-self: watched YouTube for 4 hours.

words.usageNote: 'Self-starter' in job descriptions = we won't hold your hand. Make sure you're truly comfortable with autonomy.

words.simpleMeaning: an ambitious and highly motivated person

sentences.daily: She's a real go-getter — she closes 3 deals a week.
sentences.office: I consider myself a go-getter who sets bold goals.
sentences.interview: Go-getters don't wait for opportunities — they create them.
sentences.business: The company recruited go-getters for its fast-growth team.
sentences.formal: His go-getter attitude made him a VP in just four years.
sentences.informal: Go-getter energy: 8 AM gym, 9 AM meeting, 10 PM still working.

words.usageNote: 'Go-getter' = positive label for someone driven. Avoid saying 'I'm a go-getter' — show it with evidence instead.

words.simpleMeaning: a strong enthusiasm or love for something

sentences.daily: My passion for data led me to pursue a career in analytics.
sentences.office: Passion fuels persistence when the work gets hard.
sentences.interview: What is your passion, and how does it relate to this role?
sentences.business: Her passion for education drove her to start an NGO.
sentences.formal: Passion alone is not enough — skills must match.
sentences.informal: My passion: food. My career: trying to make money to fund it.

words.usageNote: In interviews: 'I am passionate about...' Back it up with ACTION — courses, projects, or side work.

words.simpleMeaning: comparing the current state to the desired state

sentences.daily: A gap analysis revealed missing skills on the team.
sentences.office: Run a gap analysis before building a training programme.
sentences.interview: I performed a gap analysis of our digital marketing strategy.
sentences.business: The gap analysis identified 5 areas needing immediate attention.
sentences.formal: Results from the gap analysis informed the Q3 roadmap.
sentences.informal: My personal gap analysis: skills I have vs skills I need. The list is long.

words.usageNote: Gap analysis = current state vs desired state. The 'gap' = what needs to be fixed. Use it in strategy and HR.

words.simpleMeaning: skills that can be used in many different roles

sentences.daily: Leadership is one of the most transferable skills.
sentences.office: Highlight transferable skills when switching industries.
sentences.interview: My transferable skills include project management and communication.
sentences.business: Transferable skills give career changers an edge.
sentences.formal: The candidate's transferable skills outweighed the experience gap.
sentences.informal: Transferable skill: convincing people — works in sales AND at home!

words.usageNote: Transferable skills work across roles and industries. Communication, leadership, and analytical thinking top the list.

words.simpleMeaning: a structured way to answer interview questions

sentences.daily: Use the STAR method for behavioral interview questions.
sentences.office: STAR = Situation, Task, Action, Result.
sentences.interview: I practiced STAR answers for 10 common questions.
sentences.business: Strong STAR answers impress interviewers with clear examples.
sentences.formal: The STAR method helped candidates articulate accomplishments clearly.
sentences.informal: STAR method: I know the theory. Live interview: forgot everything!

words.usageNote: STAR = Situation → Task → Action → Result. Use it for: 'Tell me about a time when...' questions. Always end with RESULT.

words.simpleMeaning: pay and benefits an employee receives

sentences.daily: My expected compensation is between ₹10–12 LPA.
sentences.office: Compensation includes salary, bonuses, and benefits.
sentences.interview: Discuss compensation only after an offer is made.
sentences.business: The company offers competitive compensation and flexible hours.
sentences.formal: Compensation was benchmarked against industry standards.
sentences.informal: Compensation for my hard work: at least make the chai!

words.usageNote: 'Compensation' is more formal than 'salary.' Use it in professional negotiations. Know your market rate.

words.hindi: लाभ उठाना / दबाव का उपयोग करना
words.simpleMeaning: to use something to your advantage

sentences.daily: I leveraged my network to find this opportunity.
sentences.office: Leverage your strengths in every interview answer.
sentences.interview: Use data to leverage your argument in salary negotiations.
sentences.business: The startup leveraged social media for zero-cost marketing.
sentences.formal: The negotiator leveraged the competing offer successfully.
sentences.informal: I leveraged my seniority to get the window seat!

words.usageNote: 'Leverage' = use something you have to gain an advantage. A very useful word in business and career contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: an experienced person who guides and advises someone

sentences.daily: Find a mentor in your first job — it makes all the difference.
sentences.office: My mentor helped me navigate office politics early on.
sentences.interview: A good mentor gives honest feedback, not just praise.
sentences.business: I mentor two junior developers every week.
sentences.formal: The mentoring relationship lasted 5 years and shaped her career.
sentences.informal: My mentor told me to speak up. Still working on it!

words.usageNote: 'Mentor' = guides with wisdom and experience. 'Coach' = helps you improve specific skills. Both are valuable.

words.simpleMeaning: a group of professional contacts you build over time

sentences.daily: My professional network helped me find this role.
sentences.office: Networking is not about collecting business cards — it's about relationships.
sentences.interview: I network at conferences and on LinkedIn.
sentences.business: A strong network opens doors that skills alone cannot.
sentences.formal: She built a network of 500 professionals in her first year.
sentences.informal: Networking event: I spoke to the appetizers mostly.

words.usageNote: 'Network' as verb: 'I regularly network in my industry.' LinkedIn, alumni meets, and conferences are best places.

words.simpleMeaning: a document detailing the duties and requirements of a job

sentences.daily: Read the job description carefully before applying.
sentences.office: The job description matched my skills and experience perfectly.
sentences.interview: I tailored my resume to each job description.
sentences.business: The job description specified 5 years of experience in Python.
sentences.formal: Job descriptions often list 'must-have' and 'nice-to-have' skills.
sentences.informal: Job description says '2 years experience required' — hired a fresher anyway!

words.usageNote: Map your skills to the job description point-by-point. Match their language in your cover letter and resume.

words.simpleMeaning: a brief, persuasive description of yourself or your idea

sentences.daily: Practice your elevator pitch until it sounds natural.
sentences.office: My elevator pitch: 'I'm a data analyst who turns numbers into stories.'
sentences.interview: You have 60 seconds — make your elevator pitch count.
sentences.business: The entrepreneur delivered a compelling elevator pitch at the summit.
sentences.formal: Elevator pitches should be clear, confident, and memorable.
sentences.informal: My elevator pitch was great — until the elevator reached my floor!

words.usageNote: Elevator pitch = 30–60 second answer to 'Tell me about yourself.' Include: who you are, what you do, what you want.

words.simpleMeaning: a list of selected candidates for the final round

sentences.daily: I was shortlisted for the final round of interviews.
sentences.office: Only 5 candidates were shortlisted from 200 applicants.
sentences.interview: Tailor your resume to get shortlisted.
sentences.business: The hiring team shortlisted 10 candidates for assessment.
sentences.formal: Shortlisted candidates were notified by email.
sentences.informal: I was shortlisted for pizza toppings decision — prestigious!

words.usageNote: Getting shortlisted = making it past initial screening. The next step: interview rounds. Polish your resume!

words.simpleMeaning: an interview with multiple interviewers at once

sentences.daily: I had a panel interview with HR, the manager, and a director.
sentences.office: Panel interviews assess how you handle multiple perspectives.
sentences.interview: Address all panel members equally, not just the senior person.
sentences.business: The panel interview lasted 90 minutes and was very thorough.
sentences.formal: Panel interviews are common in government and corporate hiring.
sentences.informal: Panel of 5 people. Me: panicking. Classic panel interview.

words.usageNote: In a panel interview: make eye contact with each person. Answer the question while addressing the whole group.

words.simpleMeaning: an initial trial period at a new job

sentences.daily: My probation period is 6 months.
sentences.office: Perform your best during the probation period — it's your foundation.
sentences.interview: The company extends probation if performance is below expectations.
sentences.business: I successfully completed probation and received a glowing review.
sentences.formal: Probation performance determines confirmation as a permanent employee.
sentences.informal: Probation period: they're watching everything. ACT accordingly.

words.usageNote: During probation: be punctual, ask questions, document your work, meet goals, and BUILD TRUST.

words.simpleMeaning: a document expressing your intention to take a job or apply

sentences.daily: Submit a letter of intent with your application.
sentences.office: My letter of intent explained why I want this specific role.
sentences.interview: A strong letter of intent can outweigh a mediocre CV.
sentences.business: The letter of intent was reviewed by the department head.
sentences.formal: The university required a letter of intent for the PhD program.
sentences.informal: Letter of intent: 'I intend to work here and eat all the free food.'

words.usageNote: Letter of Intent (LOI) = your motivation to join. Different from a cover letter (which summarises your qualifications).

words.hindi: छंटनी भत्ता / नौकरी समाप्ति पर मिलने वाला मुआवज़ा
words.simpleMeaning: compensation given to an employee when their job ends

sentences.daily: He received three months' severance when the company downsized.
sentences.office: Negotiate severance terms before signing a contract.
sentences.interview: Severance packages vary by company policy and tenure.
sentences.business: The departing director received a generous severance package.
sentences.formal: Severance was paid in lieu of the notice period.
sentences.informal: Severance: the silver lining of being let go.

words.usageNote: Severance = money paid when employment is TERMINATED. Know your rights — it's usually one month per year served.

words.simpleMeaning: a recruiter who approaches people for specific job roles

sentences.daily: A headhunter reached out to me on LinkedIn.
sentences.office: Headhunters are paid by companies to find specific talent.
sentences.interview: If a headhunter contacts you, your reputation precedes you.
sentences.business: Top headhunters specialise in C-suite recruitment.
sentences.formal: The headhunter placed her in a senior role within 2 weeks.
sentences.informal: Being headhunted: feel like a celebrity. For 5 minutes, at least.

words.usageNote: Headhunters are recruiters who FIND YOU. Opposite of a job portal where you find them. Both paths work.

words.simpleMeaning: an offer made in response to another offer, usually in salary talks

sentences.daily: I made a counter offer asking for ₹2 lakh more.
sentences.office: Always be ready to negotiate and make a counter offer.
sentences.interview: The employer accepted my counter offer after a day.
sentences.business: Counter offers from the current employer should be assessed carefully.
sentences.formal: A counter offer was exchanged and a final package agreed upon.
sentences.informal: Counter offer accepted. Now my current boss wants to fight.

words.usageNote: Counter offer = your response to their initial offer. Research market rates so your counter is justified.

words.simpleMeaning: maintaining a healthy balance between work and personal life

sentences.daily: I choose companies that prioritise work-life balance.
sentences.office: Work-life balance means different things to different people.
sentences.interview: My team ensures work-life balance by respecting off-hours.
sentences.business: Poor work-life balance leads to burnout and decreased productivity.
sentences.formal: The company's work-life balance policy included flexible hours.
sentences.informal: Work-life balance: work from home + no commute = still overworking.

words.usageNote: Work-life balance is about BOUNDARIES. Set them early. 'Hustle culture' glorifies overwork — it's not sustainable.

words.simpleMeaning: to evaluate something or someone's performance or value

sentences.daily: The manager appraised each team member quarterly.
sentences.office: Self-appraise honestly before your official review.
sentences.interview: Properties are appraised by certified valuers.
sentences.business: The director appraised the project's ROI before approving it.
sentences.formal: Staff were appraised using a 360-degree feedback model.
sentences.informal: My cat appraised me and decided I was an acceptable couch.

words.usageNote: 'Appraise' = formally evaluate. 'Appraisal' = the process. 'Appraiser' = person who does it.

words.simpleMeaning: to understand something fully

sentences.daily: I read the chapter twice before I could comprehend it.
sentences.office: Good readers comprehend both meaning and tone.
sentences.interview: Did you fully comprehend the assignment instructions?
sentences.business: Students were tested on their ability to comprehend complex texts.
sentences.formal: The researcher took time to comprehend the full dataset.
sentences.informal: I comprehend the theory; the exam disagrees.

words.usageNote: Comprehend > understand. Use in formal writing. 'Reading comprehension' = how well you understand what you read.

words.simpleMeaning: to combine information from different sources into one idea

sentences.daily: A good essay synthesizes ideas from multiple sources.
sentences.office: I synthesized findings from 10 research papers.
sentences.interview: Synthesize, don't just summarize — add your own insight.
sentences.business: The report synthesized customer feedback from three markets.
sentences.formal: The student synthesized theory and practice effectively.
sentences.informal: Synthesizing information takes practice — it's not just copy-paste!

words.usageNote: Summarize = repeat. Synthesize = combine DIFFERENT ideas into a NEW insight. Synthesis is a higher-order skill.

words.simpleMeaning: a proposed explanation that needs to be tested

sentences.daily: My hypothesis is that students learn better with examples.
sentences.office: Form a hypothesis before starting any experiment.
sentences.interview: The hypothesis was tested through controlled experiments.
sentences.business: A good hypothesis must be specific and falsifiable.
sentences.formal: The research hypothesis was rejected based on the data.
sentences.informal: Hypothesis: studying the night before works. Result: does not.

words.usageNote: Hypothesis → Experiment → Data → Conclusion. The scientific method in 4 steps.

words.simpleMeaning: a reason or set of reasons to support an idea

sentences.daily: Build a strong argument with evidence, not emotion.
sentences.office: The essay's central argument was clear and convincing.
sentences.interview: What is your main argument in this assignment?
sentences.business: The student's argument was well-structured and supported.
sentences.formal: The debate required each team to present a logical argument.
sentences.informal: My argument with myself at midnight: 'Should I study?' 'Yes.' 'But...'

words.usageNote: In academic writing: an argument is a REASONED CLAIM supported by evidence. It must be logical, not emotional.

words.simpleMeaning: facts or information that support a claim

sentences.daily: Always support your argument with evidence.
sentences.office: The evidence clearly showed a link between sleep and memory.
sentences.interview: Cite your evidence with proper references.
sentences.business: Strong evidence makes a weak argument strong.
sentences.formal: The courtroom evidence was presented over three days.
sentences.informal: Evidence for why I should stay home: this couch is comfortable.

words.usageNote: In academic writing: every claim needs evidence. Evidence includes data, examples, quotes, and research findings.

words.simpleMeaning: the main argument of an essay or a research dissertation

sentences.daily: State your thesis clearly in the first paragraph.
sentences.office: Her PhD thesis challenged existing theories of memory.
sentences.interview: A strong thesis statement is specific, debatable, and clear.
sentences.business: The thesis was reviewed by three external examiners.
sentences.formal: I spent 6 months writing and rewriting my thesis.
sentences.informal: Thesis statement: crisp. Thesis submission: always late.

words.usageNote: Thesis (essay) = main argument. Thesis (postgrad) = a long research paper submitted for a degree.

words.simpleMeaning: a reference to the source of information you have used

sentences.daily: Add a citation whenever you use someone else's idea.
sentences.office: APA and MLA are two common citation styles.
sentences.interview: Missing citations leads to plagiarism penalties.
sentences.business: Each fact was supported with a proper citation.
sentences.formal: The bibliography listed 35 citations from peer-reviewed journals.
sentences.informal: Citation style: perfect in my head, always wrong on paper.

words.usageNote: Cite your sources — always. 'Give credit where credit is due.' No citation = potential plagiarism.

words.simpleMeaning: copying someone else's work and claiming it as your own

sentences.daily: Plagiarism can result in expulsion from university.
sentences.office: Always cite sources to avoid accidental plagiarism.
sentences.interview: Turnitin is a tool that detects plagiarism in submitted work.
sentences.business: Her assignment was flagged for plagiarism due to missing citations.
sentences.formal: Academic institutions take plagiarism very seriously.
sentences.informal: Plagiarism check passed: somehow my essay was 0% original, 100% copied. Oops.

words.usageNote: Plagiarism = taking credit for work you didn't create. Even unintentional plagiarism can have serious consequences.

words.simpleMeaning: to restate something in your own words

sentences.daily: Don't copy text — paraphrase it in your own words.
sentences.office: Paraphrasing shows you truly understand the material.
sentences.interview: I paraphrased the concept to explain it to my classmate.
sentences.business: The student paraphrased the source without changing the meaning.
sentences.formal: Paraphrasing is an essential academic writing skill.
sentences.informal: I paraphrased my essay so well my teacher thought it was original!

words.usageNote: Paraphrase = same idea, YOUR words. Different from summarize = shorter. Different from quote = exact words.

words.simpleMeaning: the subjects and courses taught in an educational programme

sentences.daily: The curriculum was updated to include digital literacy.
sentences.office: The school's curriculum covers STEM and arts equally.
sentences.interview: I designed a curriculum for the English speaking workshop.
sentences.business: The university curriculum was redesigned based on industry needs.
sentences.formal: Students were consulted before finalising the new curriculum.
sentences.informal: The curriculum said 'no mobile phones.' The students disagreed.

words.usageNote: Curriculum (whole programme) vs Syllabus (specific course). Curriculum = bigger, syllabus = smaller and more specific.

words.simpleMeaning: to add notes or comments to a text

sentences.daily: I annotate every book I read with my own thoughts.
sentences.office: Students were asked to annotate the passage for homework.
sentences.interview: Annotating helps you engage more deeply with the material.
sentences.business: The teacher annotated the essay with feedback.
sentences.formal: The PDF was annotated using digital highlighting tools.
sentences.informal: My annotations are 90% question marks and 10% 'what?'

words.usageNote: Annotate = write notes IN or BESIDE the text. Creates a conversation between you and the author.

words.simpleMeaning: to review and improve, or to study again before an exam

sentences.daily: I revise my notes every Sunday to retain information.
sentences.office: Revise the essay once before you submit it.
sentences.interview: Revising under pressure is harder than studying early.
sentences.business: The research team revised the paper after peer review.
sentences.formal: Students revised all 10 chapters the night before the exam.
sentences.informal: Revision plan: excellent. Execution: Netflix then panic.

words.usageNote: In UK English, 'revise' = study before an exam. In writing: revise = improve and edit your draft.

words.simpleMeaning: a brief summary of a longer academic text

sentences.daily: Write a 150-word abstract before submitting your paper.
sentences.office: I read the abstract first to decide if the paper is relevant.
sentences.interview: A strong abstract contains the problem, method, and findings.
sentences.business: The abstract was the most read part of the published paper.
sentences.formal: Each conference paper required a 200-word abstract.
sentences.informal: Read the abstract: 30 seconds. Read the full paper: 3 hours. Worth it!

words.usageNote: Abstract = what the paper is ABOUT in brief. It should answer: Why? How? What did you find? So what?

words.simpleMeaning: evaluation of academic work by other experts

sentences.daily: All our research is peer reviewed before publication.
sentences.office: Peer review improves the quality of academic work.
sentences.interview: The paper passed peer review and was published in Nature.
sentences.business: Students conducted peer reviews of each other's essays.
sentences.formal: Peer review provides critical feedback from knowledgeable readers.
sentences.informal: Peer review of my essay: 'This needs more work.' Understatement.

words.usageNote: Peer review = independent check by experts in the same field. It is the gold standard of academic quality.

words.simpleMeaning: a small, interactive class or academic session

sentences.daily: The seminar had 15 students and lots of discussion.
sentences.office: I attend a weekly seminar on machine learning.
sentences.interview: Seminars are more interactive than lectures.
sentences.business: The professor led a three-hour seminar on postcolonial theory.
sentences.formal: The seminar required students to come prepared with arguments.
sentences.informal: Seminar participation: 10% insight, 90% hoping no one calls on me.

words.usageNote: Seminar = small interactive group discussion. Lecture = one person speaking to many. Both are important.

words.simpleMeaning: to evaluate or judge something carefully

sentences.daily: Teachers assess students through assignments and exams.
sentences.office: Assess your own understanding before the test.
sentences.interview: The committee assessed the project's feasibility.
sentences.business: Students were assessed on content, structure, and clarity.
sentences.formal: The risk was assessed before the implementation began.
sentences.informal: Assessed myself as 'exam-ready.' The exam had other opinions.

words.usageNote: 'Assess' is used in academic, medical, and business contexts. More formal than 'check' or 'look at.'

words.simpleMeaning: the specific methods used in research or a project

sentences.daily: My research methodology included surveys and case studies.
sentences.office: Explain your methodology clearly in the report.
sentences.interview: A mixed-methods methodology was used in the study.
sentences.business: The methodology chapter is the backbone of a good dissertation.
sentences.formal: The research methodology was approved by the ethics committee.
sentences.informal: Methodology: 'I made it up as I went.' Research board: 'Please don't.'

words.usageNote: Methodology = the SYSTEM of methods you use. A single method vs a methodology = one tool vs a whole toolbox.

words.simpleMeaning: a long research document required for a postgraduate degree

sentences.daily: I spent 8 months writing my master's dissertation.
sentences.office: Your dissertation must demonstrate original research.
sentences.interview: Submit the dissertation draft to your supervisor first.
sentences.business: The dissertation was 20,000 words and passed with distinction.
sentences.formal: Dissertation defence is a formal oral examination of your research.
sentences.informal: Dissertation status: started, abandoned, restarted, nearly done, crying.

words.usageNote: Dissertation = major research project for a Master's or PhD. Typically 10,000–100,000 words depending on level.

words.simpleMeaning: a summary and analysis of existing research on a topic

sentences.daily: My literature review covered 50 published papers.
sentences.office: The literature review shows what is already known.
sentences.interview: A good literature review identifies gaps in current knowledge.
sentences.business: The literature review took three weeks to complete.
sentences.formal: Reviewers praised the comprehensive literature review.
sentences.informal: Literature review: reading 50 papers to say 'we need more research.'

words.usageNote: Lit review = map of existing knowledge. It shows WHERE your research fits and WHY it is needed.

words.simpleMeaning: a conclusion drawn from evidence

sentences.daily: Draw a logical inference from the given data.
sentences.office: Critical reading involves making inferences between the lines.
sentences.interview: The researcher's inference was supported by three datasets.
sentences.business: Students must make inferences when information is implied.
sentences.formal: The inference was validated through follow-up experimentation.
sentences.informal: Inference: they stopped replying = they're not interested. Mostly correct.

words.usageNote: Inference = conclusion reached through REASONING. Different from assumption, which may have no evidence.

words.simpleMeaning: a detailed evaluation or analysis of something

sentences.daily: Write a critique of the article, not just a summary.
sentences.office: Her critique of the policy was sharp and evidence-based.
