sentences.interview: I critiqued three case studies for my management course.
sentences.business: A fair critique acknowledges both strengths and weaknesses.
sentences.formal: The design critique led to significant improvements in the product.
sentences.informal: Critique: 'Your essay needs work.' Me: 'Which parts?' 'All of it.'

words.usageNote: Critique ≠ criticize. A critique is ANALYTICAL and BALANCED — it evaluates fairly, not just negatively.

words.simpleMeaning: a plan showing the main points of something

sentences.daily: Create an outline before writing your essay.
sentences.office: I outlined my presentation into three main sections.
sentences.interview: A clear outline keeps your essay focused and structured.
sentences.business: The project outline was approved by the supervisor.
sentences.formal: Students submitted a chapter outline before beginning the draft.
sentences.informal: Outline: 5 minutes. Actual essay: 5 hours. Still worth doing.

words.usageNote: Outline first, write second. An outline prevents you from going off-track and losing marks for structure.

words.simpleMeaning: to reference the source of information you have used

sentences.daily: Always cite your sources in academic writing.
sentences.office: I cited 20 peer-reviewed articles in my paper.
sentences.interview: Did you cite the data or did you make it up?
sentences.business: All cited works must appear in the reference list.
sentences.formal: The student cited APA style correctly throughout the essay.
sentences.informal: Forgot to cite one source — marked down 5%. Lesson learned.

words.usageNote: Cite = point to where your information came from. No citation = no credibility, or worse, plagiarism.

words.simpleMeaning: a set of criteria used to grade or evaluate work

sentences.daily: Check the rubric before starting your assignment.
sentences.office: The rubric weights creativity at 30% and content at 40%.
sentences.interview: My work scored full marks against every rubric criterion.
sentences.business: The professor shared the rubric at the start of the semester.
sentences.formal: Rubric-based grading ensures fairness and transparency.
sentences.informal: I read the rubric AFTER submitting. Never again.

words.usageNote: A rubric breaks a grade into specific, measurable criteria. It removes subjectivity. Always read it first!

words.simpleMeaning: a half-year academic term

sentences.daily: I'm in my second semester of the MBA programme.
sentences.office: The semester starts in July and ends in November.
sentences.interview: Each semester has its own set of courses and deadlines.
sentences.business: Results were published two weeks after the semester ended.
sentences.formal: The exchange student joined in the second semester.
sentences.informal: My semester plan: study hard. Reality: coffee and last-minute panic.

words.usageNote: Most universities: 2 semesters per year. Some: 3 trimesters. Know your academic calendar — it prevents surprises.

words.simpleMeaning: a private teacher who helps one-on-one

sentences.daily: I hire a tutor for mathematics every week.
sentences.office: My English tutor helped me become fluent in 6 months.
sentences.interview: She tutors three students in the evening.
sentences.business: A good tutor asks questions — they don't just explain.
sentences.formal: The tutor prepared personalised study materials for each student.
sentences.informal: My English tutor: this app. My math tutor: also this app!

words.usageNote: Tutor = personal, one-on-one teaching. More focused than a classroom teacher. Great for specific skill gaps.

words.simpleMeaning: a task given to a student to complete outside class

sentences.daily: I have three assignments due this week.
sentences.office: Submit your assignment by midnight on Friday.
sentences.interview: This assignment asks you to compare two business models.
sentences.business: The assignment was weighted at 40% of the final grade.
sentences.formal: All assignments must be submitted via the online portal.
sentences.informal: Assignment submitted at 11:59 PM. Grace under pressure.

words.usageNote: Assignment (task given to you) vs project (larger, often group work) vs exam (in-class test). Know the difference.

words.simpleMeaning: activities outside the regular academic programme

sentences.daily: Extracurricular activities build leadership and social skills.
sentences.office: I led the drama club as an extracurricular commitment.
sentences.interview: Employers value extracurricular achievements on resumes.
sentences.business: The school offered 15 extracurricular clubs and sports.
sentences.formal: Extracurricular experience was factored into university admissions.
sentences.informal: Extracurricular activities: I watched Netflix. Does that count?

words.usageNote: Extracurricular = activities beyond formal study. Sports, clubs, volunteering, events. List them on your CV!

words.simpleMeaning: a student's average academic grade

sentences.daily: My GPA was 3.8 out of 4.0 in my undergraduate degree.
sentences.office: A high GPA opens doors to top universities and scholarships.
sentences.interview: Some employers still ask for GPA in entry-level hiring.
sentences.business: I maintained a 9.2 CGPA throughout my engineering course.
sentences.formal: GPA requirements vary by university and programme.
sentences.informal: Studying hard for a 10 GPA. Got an 8. Still proud!

words.usageNote: GPA (US) = 4.0 scale. CGPA (India) = 10.0 scale. Both measure academic performance across all subjects.

words.simpleMeaning: a short-term work experience while studying

sentences.daily: I did a 3-month internship at a marketing agency.
sentences.office: Internships give students real-world experience.
sentences.interview: My internship was unpaid, but the learning was invaluable.
sentences.business: The company hired 5 interns who performed well.
sentences.formal: The internship programme offered mentorship and live projects.
sentences.informal: Internship task: make tea AND do analysis. Did both.

words.usageNote: Internships are stepping stones. Even unpaid ones build your portfolio, network, and resume. Take them seriously.

words.simpleMeaning: the last date or time by which something must be done

sentences.daily: My assignment deadline is tomorrow night at 11:59 PM.
sentences.office: Always start before the deadline — never at it.
sentences.interview: Missing deadlines has academic and professional consequences.
sentences.business: The student requested an extension to the submission deadline.
sentences.formal: All project deadlines were tracked on the shared calendar.
sentences.informal: Deadline: 11:59 PM. Started: 11:30 PM. Finished: 11:58 PM. Warrior.

words.usageNote: Missing academic deadlines = grade penalties. Missing professional deadlines = reputation damage. Respect both!

words.simpleMeaning: grading criteria with clear descriptions of performance levels

sentences.daily: I designed a rubric for the oral presentation assessment.
sentences.office: Always read the rubric — it tells you exactly how you'll be graded.
sentences.interview: The rubric had four levels: excellent, good, satisfactory, and failing.
sentences.business: The rubric was shared a week before the assignment was due.
sentences.formal: Rubric-based grading gave students clear feedback on improvement.
sentences.informal: Finally read the rubric. Turns out content was only 30%. Formatting was 70%. Oops.

words.usageNote: Rubric = a table showing what you need to do to earn each grade. It's your roadmap to a top score.

words.simpleMeaning: the ability to analyse and evaluate information objectively

sentences.daily: Critical thinking is the most important skill for the 21st century.
sentences.office: I apply critical thinking to evaluate news and information.
sentences.interview: Universities aim to develop students' critical thinking skills.
sentences.business: Critical thinking helped identify flaws in the study design.
sentences.formal: The interview tested candidates' critical thinking through case studies.
sentences.informal: Critical thinking: questioning everything, even this sentence.

words.usageNote: Critical thinking = don't accept information at face value. Ask: What's the evidence? Who says so? What's missing?

words.simpleMeaning: financial support given to students to study

sentences.daily: I received a full scholarship to study engineering.
sentences.office: Scholarships reduce the financial burden of higher education.
sentences.interview: Apply early — most scholarships have limited seats.
sentences.business: The scholarship covered tuition, accommodation, and travel.
sentences.formal: The merit scholarship was awarded to the top 10 students.
sentences.informal: Applied for 10 scholarships. Got 1. Worth every application!

words.usageNote: Scholarship (merit-based) vs bursary (need-based) vs grant (for research). Know which type you're applying for.

words.simpleMeaning: the process of getting better at something over time

sentences.daily: There is a steep learning curve when starting a new job.
sentences.office: Every skill has a learning curve — embrace it.
sentences.interview: I got through the learning curve for Python in about 3 weeks.
sentences.business: New software always has a short but steep learning curve.
sentences.formal: Patience and practice flatten any learning curve over time.
sentences.informal: My learning curve for cooking: still a very steep uphill road!

words.usageNote: 'Steep learning curve' = hard to learn quickly. 'Shallow curve' = easy to pick up. Embrace both with patience.

words.simpleMeaning: relating to mental processes like thinking, learning, and memory

sentences.daily: Exercise improves cognitive performance in students.
sentences.office: Cognitive load affects how much you can learn at one time.
sentences.interview: Sleep deprivation impairs cognitive function significantly.
sentences.business: The test measured cognitive skills, not just subject knowledge.
sentences.formal: Cognitive science explores how the human mind processes information.
sentences.informal: My cognitive function peaks at 10 AM and crashes at 3 PM.

words.usageNote: Cognitive = related to HOW THE MIND WORKS. Cognitive skills include memory, attention, problem-solving, and reasoning.

words.simpleMeaning: to delay doing something you should do

sentences.daily: I procrastinate when a task feels overwhelming.
sentences.office: Stop procrastinating — start with just 5 minutes.
sentences.interview: Procrastinating costs more stress than the task itself.
sentences.business: The student procrastinated for two weeks and then panicked.
sentences.formal: Chronic procrastination is linked to anxiety, not laziness.
sentences.informal: I procrastinate on procrastinating. Meta.

words.usageNote: Procrastination is not laziness — it's often fear or perfectionism. Break tasks into tiny steps to overcome it.

words.simpleMeaning: a list of all sources used in a piece of writing

sentences.daily: Add a bibliography at the end of your research paper.
sentences.office: A bibliography is mandatory for all academic submissions.
sentences.interview: I formatted my bibliography in APA style.
sentences.business: The bibliography listed 45 sources from 8 countries.
sentences.formal: The professor checked the bibliography for citation accuracy.
sentences.informal: My bibliography was longer than my essay — I over-researched!

words.usageNote: Bibliography (all sources you read) vs reference list (only sources you cited). Know the difference!

words.simpleMeaning: a self-contained unit of a course

sentences.daily: I completed 6 modules in the first semester.
sentences.office: Each module has its own assignments and final assessment.
sentences.interview: The data science module was my favourite of the year.
sentences.business: Modules can be elective (chosen) or compulsory.
sentences.formal: The online platform delivers each module through video and quiz.
sentences.informal: Module on statistics: loved the graphs, hated the math.

words.usageNote: Module = one course/subject within a larger programme. You get separate marks for each module.

words.simpleMeaning: a teaching session for a small group; or an instructional guide

sentences.daily: I watch YouTube tutorials to learn new software skills.
sentences.office: The tutorial covered the first three chapters of the textbook.
sentences.interview: Online tutorials make self-study much easier.
sentences.business: The professor's tutorial group met every Thursday afternoon.
sentences.formal: Step-by-step tutorials are available on the university portal.
sentences.informal: Tutorial: 10 minutes long. Time I spent: 3 hours rewatching it.

words.usageNote: Tutorial (academic) = small teaching session. Tutorial (tech) = step-by-step instructional guide. Both are useful!

words.simpleMeaning: the ability to remember and keep information

sentences.daily: Spaced repetition improves information retention significantly.
sentences.office: Sleep plays a critical role in long-term memory retention.
sentences.interview: Regular review boosts retention of vocabulary words.
sentences.business: The training focused on knowledge retention through active recall.
sentences.formal: Student retention rates improved after the new curriculum was introduced.
sentences.informal: My retention: 100% for movie quotes, 0% for exam formulas.

words.usageNote: Retention = remembering information LONG-TERM. Sleep, revision, and practice all improve retention. No magic shortcut.

words.simpleMeaning: thinking logically about patterns and relationships, not concrete facts

sentences.daily: Abstract reasoning is tested in most aptitude exams.
sentences.office: Strong abstract reasoning is essential for problem-solving roles.
sentences.interview: Practice pattern-based questions to develop abstract reasoning.
sentences.business: The psychometric test included a 20-minute abstract reasoning section.
sentences.formal: Abstract reasoning ability was assessed during the selection process.
sentences.informal: Abstract reasoning question: which shape comes next? Me: yes.

words.usageNote: Abstract reasoning = thinking about rules and patterns, not specific content. Tested in IQ tests and aptitude exams.

words.simpleMeaning: the act of handing in work for assessment

sentences.daily: The submission portal closes at midnight tonight.
sentences.office: Late submissions are penalised by 10% per day.
sentences.interview: I submitted my assignment on time and received full marks.
sentences.business: Submissions must be in PDF format with cover page.
sentences.formal: All submissions are automatically checked for plagiarism.
sentences.informal: Submission: 11:58 PM. Server crash: 11:59 PM. My life in chaos.

words.usageNote: 'Submission' in UK/India universities = turning in your work. 'Extension' = extra time when you need more.

words.simpleMeaning: to watch over students during an exam

sentences.daily: Three teachers invigilated the three-hour exam.
sentences.office: The hall was silent while the invigilator checked the room.
sentences.interview: Invigilators ensure exams are conducted fairly.
sentences.business: Only certified staff are allowed to invigilate university exams.
sentences.formal: The invigilator confiscated a mobile phone during the paper.
sentences.informal: Invigilator walked past. Heart rate: 200 BPM. Reason: innocent.

words.usageNote: Invigilate (UK English) = proctor (US English). The person who watches during an exam to prevent cheating.

words.simpleMeaning: learning by memorizing without deeper understanding

sentences.daily: Rote learning helps with vocabulary but not with essays.
sentences.office: India's education system has long relied on rote learning.
sentences.interview: Rote learning is effective for facts, not for application.
sentences.business: Modern education encourages understanding over rote learning.
sentences.formal: Students who rely only on rote learning struggle in practical exams.
sentences.informal: Rote learning: I know ALL the words. I understand NONE of them.

words.usageNote: Rote learning = memorising WITHOUT understanding. Useful for tables, vocabulary, dates. Risky for exams that test application.

words.simpleMeaning: the method of testing yourself to strengthen memory

sentences.daily: Active recall is the most effective study technique.
sentences.office: Use flashcards for active recall after reading a chapter.
sentences.interview: I scored 90% because I practised active recall every day.
sentences.business: Active recall forces your brain to retrieve information.
sentences.formal: Research shows active recall outperforms passive re-reading.
sentences.informal: Active recall vs highlighting: one works, one is colourful.

words.usageNote: Active recall = close the book and TRY to remember. This forces your brain to strengthen the memory pathway.

words.simpleMeaning: reviewing material at increasing intervals over time

sentences.daily: Spaced repetition is the science behind Anki flashcards.
sentences.office: I use spaced repetition to memorise vocabulary efficiently.
sentences.interview: Review material at Day 1, 3, 7, 14, 30 — that's spaced repetition.
sentences.business: Spaced repetition takes longer but produces permanent memory.
sentences.formal: Language learners who use spaced repetition progress 40% faster.
sentences.informal: Spaced repetition: studying now so future-me suffers less. Genius.

words.usageNote: Spaced repetition = review right before you forget. It's the most efficient way to transfer info to long-term memory.

words.simpleMeaning: a teaching method based on asking and answering questions

sentences.daily: The Socratic method challenges students to think, not just recall.
sentences.office: Law schools often use the Socratic method in classrooms.
sentences.interview: A good teacher uses the Socratic method to guide discovery.
sentences.business: The Socratic method develops critical thinking through dialogue.
sentences.formal: The professor used the Socratic method to help students form conclusions.
sentences.informal: Socratic method: teacher asks questions. Student panics. Learning happens.

words.usageNote: Socratic method = learning through QUESTIONS, not answers. Named after the Greek philosopher Socrates.

words.hindi: मेटाकॉग्निशन / स्वयं की सोच के बारे में सोचना
words.simpleMeaning: thinking about your own thinking and learning process

sentences.daily: Metacognition improves how efficiently you study.
sentences.office: Ask yourself: 'Do I really understand this, or just think I do?'
sentences.interview: Metacognition is the key difference between good and great students.
sentences.business: Metacognitive learners know WHEN to slow down and revisit.
sentences.formal: Metacognition was found to be a top predictor of academic success.
sentences.informal: Metacognition: knowing you don't know. A superpower.

words.usageNote: Metacognition = awareness and control of your OWN learning. Plan, monitor, and evaluate how you study.

words.simpleMeaning: stress or fear before or during an exam

sentences.daily: Exam anxiety is normal and can be managed.
sentences.office: Deep breathing helps reduce exam anxiety before a paper.
sentences.interview: I overcame exam anxiety through mock tests and preparation.
sentences.business: Severe exam anxiety can impair even well-prepared students.
sentences.formal: The university offered counselling sessions for exam anxiety.
sentences.informal: Exam anxiety: body in the hall, brain on vacation.

words.usageNote: Manage exam anxiety with: good preparation, sleep, breathing exercises, mock tests, and positive self-talk.

words.hindi: सावधान / बारीकी से काम करने वाला
words.simpleMeaning: extremely careful and precise

sentences.daily: She is meticulous about formatting every document.
sentences.office: My meticulous approach to testing caught the critical bug.
sentences.interview: A surgeon must be meticulous in every procedure.
sentences.business: The meticulous report was praised by all stakeholders.
sentences.formal: His meticulous attention to detail set him apart.
sentences.informal: I am meticulous about coffee — three sugars, exact temperature.

words.usageNote: Meticulous = extremely careful about small details. Often a positive quality in professional and academic contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: fluent, persuasive, and well-expressed

sentences.daily: She gave an eloquent speech that moved the audience.
sentences.office: His eloquent writing style impressed the editorial board.
sentences.interview: Being eloquent in English opens doors in professional life.
sentences.business: The eloquent presentation earned a standing ovation.
sentences.formal: She was eloquent, confident, and utterly persuasive.
sentences.informal: I'm eloquent in my mind. Out loud: very different story.

words.usageNote: Eloquent = speaking or writing in a clear, moving, and well-expressed way. A very powerful compliment.

words.simpleMeaning: continuing despite difficulties or opposition

sentences.daily: Persistent effort is the key to mastering any language.
sentences.office: She was persistent in chasing her dream despite setbacks.
sentences.interview: His persistent follow-up secured the deal.
sentences.business: The persistent student improved her grade from C to A.
sentences.formal: Persistent complaints from customers led to a product redesign.
sentences.informal: Persistent: me asking mom if we have food every 5 minutes.

words.usageNote: Persistent = keeps going even when it's hard. Persistence = the noun. 'Keep going' in one word.

words.simpleMeaning: able to adapt to many different functions

sentences.daily: She is a versatile professional — design, writing, and coding.
sentences.office: Python is a versatile programming language for any domain.
sentences.interview: A versatile player is valuable to any sports team.
sentences.business: Versatile employees are promoted faster in dynamic companies.
sentences.formal: The versatile candidate impressed with her range of skills.
sentences.informal: Chapati is versatile — sabji, roll, or just with butter!

words.usageNote: Versatile = useful in MANY different ways. A versatile skill set makes you more employable and promotable.

words.hindi: सुसंगत / तार्किक रूप से जुड़ा हुआ
words.simpleMeaning: logically connected and easy to understand

sentences.daily: Your essay must be coherent from start to finish.
sentences.office: A coherent strategy requires alignment across all teams.
sentences.interview: The proposal was well-organised and completely coherent.
sentences.business: His explanation was so coherent that even beginners understood.
sentences.formal: The coherent argument was easy to follow and accept.
sentences.informal: My explanation made perfect sense. Not coherent to anyone else.

words.usageNote: Coherent (ideas connect logically) vs Cohesive (ideas flow smoothly). Both are needed in good writing.

words.simpleMeaning: dealing with things realistically and practically

sentences.daily: Take a pragmatic approach to solving this problem.
sentences.office: She is a pragmatic leader who focuses on what works.
sentences.interview: Be pragmatic — not every decision can be perfect.
sentences.business: The company made a pragmatic choice to cut costs.
sentences.formal: The pragmatic solution was cheaper and faster to implement.
sentences.informal: Pragmatic decision: skip the gym today, sleep is better.

words.usageNote: Pragmatic = focused on what actually works, not ideals. 'A pragmatic solution' = one that is realistic, not just pretty.

words.simpleMeaning: introducing new and creative ideas

sentences.daily: The company is known for its innovative products.
sentences.office: I brought an innovative approach to customer onboarding.
sentences.interview: An innovative mindset drives growth in any organisation.
sentences.business: The innovative campaign reached 10 million users on Day 1.
sentences.formal: Innovative thinkers challenge the status quo.
sentences.informal: My most innovative idea: a mute button for loud colleagues.

words.usageNote: 'Innovative' is overused — back it up with a specific example of what you actually created or changed.

words.simpleMeaning: relating to long-term planning and goals

sentences.daily: Every decision in business should be strategic.
sentences.office: Strategic thinking is what separates managers from leaders.
sentences.interview: I took a strategic break to rebuild my skills.
sentences.business: A strategic partnership opened new markets in Southeast Asia.
sentences.formal: Strategic hiring was critical to the company's scale-up plan.
sentences.informal: Strategic plan for weekend: rest. Actual plan: ruin it with procrastination.

words.usageNote: Strategic = done with clear PURPOSE and LONG-TERM thinking. Not just smart — deliberately planned for the future.

words.simpleMeaning: open, honest, and not secretive

sentences.daily: Be transparent with your team about project challenges.
sentences.office: Transparent leadership builds trust quickly.
sentences.interview: I am transparent about my progress and blockers.
sentences.business: The company's transparent pricing model won customer loyalty.
sentences.formal: Transparent communication was cited as a company strength.
sentences.informal: Transparent: I told them I didn't finish. They appreciated it.

words.usageNote: 'Be transparent about...' = openly share information. Transparency in business = honesty with customers and employees.

words.simpleMeaning: able to understand and share others' feelings

sentences.daily: A good leader is empathetic and listens actively.
sentences.office: She is empathetic — she always understands how I feel.
sentences.interview: Being empathetic is essential in customer service roles.
sentences.business: Empathetic managers have lower staff turnover rates.
sentences.formal: The empathetic response calmed the upset customer immediately.
sentences.informal: I try to be empathetic. My face doesn't always agree.

words.usageNote: Empathetic = you FEEL and UNDERSTAND what others are going through. Different from sympathetic = you feel FOR them.

words.simpleMeaning: holding firmly to something; very determined

sentences.daily: Tenacious learners succeed even without natural talent.
sentences.office: She was tenacious in her pursuit of the scholarship.
sentences.interview: His tenacious negotiation style secured the best terms.
sentences.business: A tenacious entrepreneur turns every failure into fuel.
sentences.formal: The tenacious team completed the project despite setbacks.
sentences.informal: Tenacious: me with the last piece of gulab jamun.

words.usageNote: Tenacious = will NOT let go. A very strong, positive word. Use it for persistence in the face of real obstacles.

words.simpleMeaning: expressing much in few words

sentences.daily: Write a concise summary — no more than 100 words.
sentences.office: His emails are always concise and easy to act on.
sentences.interview: Be concise in interviews — say it clearly, then stop.
sentences.business: A concise report saves everyone's time.
sentences.formal: The concise presentation was praised for its clarity.
sentences.informal: Concise: me explaining plans. Not concise: me telling a story.

words.usageNote: Concise = short + complete. 'Eliminate words that add length but not meaning.' A vital professional writing skill.

words.simpleMeaning: working hard and carefully

sentences.daily: She is a diligent student who reviews notes every day.
sentences.office: Diligent employees rarely need to be reminded of their tasks.
sentences.interview: My diligent preparation helped me ace the interview.
sentences.business: The diligent team delivered the project two days early.
sentences.formal: Diligent verification prevented a costly mistake.
sentences.informal: Diligent study? Define diligent. Define study.

words.usageNote: Diligent = steady, careful, and hard-working. Strong resume word. Back it up: 'I diligently reviewed every report before submission.'

words.simpleMeaning: very clear and easy to understand

sentences.daily: The professor gave a lucid explanation of quantum theory.
sentences.office: Write in a lucid style so all readers can understand.
sentences.interview: Her lucid summary helped the team make a quick decision.
sentences.business: The report was praised for its lucid and precise language.
sentences.formal: A lucid argument is easier to accept and act on.
sentences.informal: Lucid: my writing after coffee. Not lucid: before coffee.

words.usageNote: Lucid = something that is immediately, effortlessly clear. Use for explanations, arguments, and writing style.

words.simpleMeaning: absolutely necessary; impossible to do without

sentences.daily: Good communication skills are indispensable in any career.
sentences.office: She made herself indispensable by mastering every process.
sentences.interview: Strong documentation is indispensable for project handover.
sentences.business: The team lead was indispensable during the crisis.
sentences.formal: Indispensable team members are rewarded and promoted.
sentences.informal: My phone is indispensable. My brain agrees reluctantly.

words.usageNote: 'Make yourself indispensable' = become so valuable they can't afford to let you go. A career strategy.

words.hindi: विपुल / बहुत कुछ उत्पन्न करने वाला
words.simpleMeaning: producing much work or many results

sentences.daily: She is a prolific writer who publishes three books a year.
sentences.office: Prolific coders commit hundreds of lines of clean code daily.
sentences.interview: The prolific researcher published 50 papers in a decade.
sentences.business: Prolific creators find inspiration everywhere.
sentences.formal: His prolific output made him the most cited author in his field.
sentences.informal: Prolific napper: I am. Prolific worker: aspirational.

words.usageNote: Prolific = produces a LOT. Use for writers, creators, scientists, or artists with a large body of work.

words.simpleMeaning: more important than anything else

sentences.daily: Customer safety is of paramount importance.
sentences.office: Accuracy is paramount when handling financial data.
sentences.interview: Trust is paramount in a client-consultant relationship.
sentences.business: Communication was identified as the paramount success factor.
sentences.formal: Following protocol is paramount during a medical emergency.
sentences.informal: Paramount importance: passing the exam. Secondary: everything else.

words.usageNote: 'Paramount' = the MOST important thing above all else. 'Safety is paramount' is a classic formal usage.

words.simpleMeaning: very small or insufficient in quantity

sentences.daily: She survived on a meagre salary of ₹8,000 a month.
sentences.office: The meagre budget forced creative problem-solving.
sentences.interview: A meagre portion of the research covered this topic.
sentences.business: Resources were meagre, but the team delivered exceptional work.
sentences.formal: The charity struggled with meagre funding from donors.
sentences.informal: Meagre meal plan: dal-chawal again. Every. Single. Day.

words.usageNote: Meagre = not enough — barely what is needed. A slightly negative word, conveying insufficient quantity or quality.

words.simpleMeaning: unreasonably high in price

sentences.daily: The hotel charged an exorbitant rate during the holidays.
sentences.office: Exorbitant fuel prices hurt the working class the most.
sentences.interview: The legal fees were exorbitant for a small business.
sentences.business: Investors avoided the project due to exorbitant initial costs.
sentences.formal: The exorbitant price of urban real estate shocked first-time buyers.
sentences.informal: Delivery charge: exorbitant. Ordering anyway: always.

words.usageNote: Exorbitant = WAY TOO EXPENSIVE. Stronger than 'expensive'. Use when the price is unfair or shocking.

words.simpleMeaning: so good it deserves to be copied

sentences.daily: Her exemplary performance earned her the Employee of the Year award.
sentences.office: He showed exemplary leadership during the crisis.
sentences.interview: The project was completed with exemplary attention to detail.
sentences.business: Exemplary service leads to loyal, referring customers.
sentences.formal: The student's exemplary effort was praised in the school assembly.
sentences.informal: Exemplary punctuality means arriving 5 minutes early. I aspire.

words.usageNote: Exemplary = so good it sets the EXAMPLE for others. A high-level compliment in professional settings.

words.simpleMeaning: having more than one possible meaning; unclear

sentences.daily: Avoid ambiguous language in legal documents.
sentences.office: The question was ambiguous — two answers were correct.
sentences.interview: Her response was ambiguous — I'm still not sure what she meant.
sentences.business: Ambiguous instructions led to three different interpretations.
sentences.formal: The ambiguous clause in the contract created a dispute.
sentences.informal: Ambiguous text: 'Let's eat, kids' or 'Let's eat kids'. Commas save lives.

words.usageNote: Ambiguous = could mean two or more things. Always clarify ambiguous information in professional settings.

words.simpleMeaning: not needed; repeating information unnecessarily; laid off

sentences.daily: Cut redundant sentences from your essay for clarity.
sentences.office: He was made redundant when the company downsized.
sentences.interview: Remove redundant steps from the workflow to save time.
sentences.business: The new software made several manual roles redundant.
sentences.formal: Redundant code wastes processing power and slows systems.
sentences.informal: 'Please RSVP if you're coming' — RSVP already means that. Redundant!

words.usageNote: Two meanings: 1) Repetitive/unnecessary. 2) UK job term: your role was eliminated. Both are important to know.

words.simpleMeaning: covering everything completely

sentences.daily: This is a comprehensive guide to English grammar.
sentences.office: The comprehensive report covered all aspects of the project.
sentences.interview: A comprehensive approach to health includes mind and body.
sentences.business: Comprehensive training reduced errors by 35%.
sentences.formal: The comprehensive insurance plan covered all medical scenarios.
sentences.informal: Comprehensive study notes: 200 pages. Time to read: never.

words.usageNote: Comprehensive = COVERS EVERYTHING. Use when you want to emphasise completeness and thoroughness.

words.simpleMeaning: large in size, value, or importance

sentences.daily: There was a substantial improvement after the training.
sentences.office: The company made a substantial investment in R&D.
sentences.interview: She provided substantial evidence to support her claim.
sentences.business: A substantial portion of the budget was allocated to marketing.
sentences.formal: Substantial changes were made to the original plan.
sentences.informal: That was a very substantial meal. I cannot move.

words.usageNote: Substantial = MEANINGFULLY large, not just 'big'. More formal than 'big'. Great for academic and business writing.

words.simpleMeaning: famous for something negative

sentences.daily: He is notorious for missing every deadline.
sentences.office: The company became notorious for poor customer service.
sentences.interview: That road is notorious for accidents during monsoon.
sentences.business: The notorious bug caused the entire system to crash.
sentences.formal: The notorious executive was removed after the scandal broke.
sentences.informal: I'm notorious in my family for forgetting birthdays. Sorry.

words.usageNote: Notorious = famous but for BAD reasons. 'Famous' vs 'Notorious': one is positive, the other is negative.

words.simpleMeaning: certain to happen; impossible to avoid

sentences.daily: Growth and change are inevitable parts of life.
sentences.office: It was inevitable that prices would rise post-lockdown.
sentences.interview: If you don't revise, failure is nearly inevitable.
sentences.business: Market correction was seen as inevitable by analysts.
sentences.formal: Conflict was inevitable given the two teams' different goals.
sentences.informal: Rain on exam day: inevitable. Umbrella at home: also inevitable.

words.usageNote: 'It was inevitable' = it was going to happen no matter what. A strong, final-sounding word.

words.simpleMeaning: no longer useful or in use

sentences.daily: Fax machines have become obsolete in most offices.
sentences.office: Paper maps became obsolete after GPS was developed.
sentences.interview: Upskill regularly or your expertise will become obsolete.
sentences.business: Several job roles became obsolete after automation.
sentences.formal: The obsolete software was replaced with a cloud-based system.
sentences.informal: My phone charger: three years old. Obsolete? Cable company thinks so.

words.usageNote: Obsolete = still exists but no longer used or needed. Technology moves fast — keep learning or risk becoming obsolete.

words.simpleMeaning: to examine something very carefully

sentences.daily: Scrutinize every clause before signing a contract.
sentences.office: Investors scrutinized the startup's financial projections.
sentences.interview: The committee scrutinized all applications thoroughly.
sentences.business: The report was scrutinized for accuracy before publication.
sentences.formal: The new policy was scrutinized by the media and the public.
sentences.informal: I scrutinized the menu for 20 minutes and ordered dal makhani anyway.

words.simpleMeaning: of critical importance in a process

sentences.daily: This is a pivotal moment for the company's future.
sentences.office: Her pivotal contribution changed the direction of the project.
sentences.interview: The pivotal decision was made by the board in Q3.
sentences.business: Pivotal hires are those who elevate an entire team's performance.
sentences.formal: The pivotal match was won in the final 10 minutes.
sentences.informal: Pivotal exam tomorrow. Sleep or revision? Decided: both. Neither done.

words.usageNote: Pivotal = at the center of everything; the moment that changes the direction. Very powerful in formal writing.

words.simpleMeaning: honest and direct, even about difficult topics

sentences.daily: Please give me candid feedback on my presentation.
sentences.office: She was candid about the challenges the project faced.
sentences.interview: A candid conversation saved the team from a bigger conflict.
sentences.business: I appreciate candid colleagues more than polite ones.
sentences.formal: The CEO gave a candid assessment of the company's performance.
sentences.informal: Candid feedback: 'Your cooking is... improving.' It's not.

words.usageNote: Candid = honest and direct, even when the truth is uncomfortable. 'Candid feedback' ≠ brutal — it's kind but honest.

words.simpleMeaning: practical rather than idealistic

sentences.daily: We need a pragmatic plan, not a perfect one.
sentences.office: She took a pragmatic approach to fixing the broken process.
sentences.interview: Pragmatic problem-solvers move faster than idealists.
sentences.business: The pragmatic compromise satisfied both parties.
sentences.formal: A pragmatic leader delivers results despite imperfect conditions.
sentences.informal: Pragmatic decision: order in. Idealistic plan: cook from scratch.

words.usageNote: Pragmatic = focused on WHAT WORKS rather than what's ideal or perfect. Essential quality in fast-moving environments.

words.simpleMeaning: the ability to understand and share another's feelings

sentences.daily: Empathy is the most human leadership quality.
sentences.office: Empathy builds deeper customer relationships.
sentences.interview: I listen with empathy before offering solutions.
sentences.business: High-empathy leaders build loyal, motivated teams.
sentences.formal: The nurse showed remarkable empathy with anxious patients.
sentences.informal: Empathy: understanding that everyone's Wi-Fi pain is equally real.

words.usageNote: Empathy (feel WITH someone) vs Sympathy (feel FOR someone). Leaders need empathy; friends offer both.

words.simpleMeaning: confident in stating opinions and claiming rights

sentences.daily: Be assertive — say what you need clearly and respectfully.
sentences.office: Assertive communication prevents misunderstandings.
sentences.interview: She assertively negotiated a 20% salary increase.
sentences.business: Assertive employees are respected by peers and managers.
sentences.formal: The assertive presenter held the room's attention throughout.
sentences.informal: Assertive: 'I need this by Friday.' Not assertive: 'Maybe... if you have time... no pressure...'

words.usageNote: Assertive = confident and clear, without being aggressive. The middle ground between passive and aggressive communication.

words.simpleMeaning: happening naturally without planning

sentences.daily: Her spontaneous laugh was infectious and real.
sentences.office: I made a spontaneous decision to apply for the job.
sentences.interview: Spontaneous gestures of kindness mean the most.
sentences.business: The spontaneous team outing boosted morale more than any event.
sentences.formal: His spontaneous performance won the audience over completely.
sentences.informal: Spontaneous road trip at midnight: the best and worst idea ever.

words.usageNote: Spontaneous = not planned in advance, happening naturally. Can be positive (fun, genuine) or risky (impulsive).

words.simpleMeaning: the capacity to recover quickly from difficulties

sentences.daily: Resilience is not about avoiding failure — it's about recovering from it.
sentences.office: I developed resilience through repeated challenges.
sentences.interview: Team resilience during the crisis was extraordinary.
sentences.business: Resilience training is now standard in many companies.
sentences.formal: Emotional resilience separates average performers from great ones.
sentences.informal: Resilience: bouncing back from Monday every single week.

words.usageNote: Resilience ≠ never struggling. It means you BOUNCE BACK after you do. Build it through small, hard challenges.

words.simpleMeaning: remarkably great in extent or ability

sentences.daily: Mozart showed prodigious musical talent at age 4.
sentences.office: The prodigious effort of the team delivered an impossible result.
sentences.interview: She wrote a prodigious amount of code in just two days.
sentences.business: The scientist's prodigious memory helped her win debates.
sentences.formal: The startup showed prodigious growth in its first quarter.
sentences.informal: My prodigious appetite is my most admirable quality.

words.usageNote: Prodigious = impressively large or remarkable in some way. Often used for talent, effort, or achievement.

words.simpleMeaning: having a sharp and clear understanding of things

sentences.daily: She made an astute observation that changed our strategy.
sentences.office: An astute investor reads market signals others miss.
sentences.interview: His astute leadership guided the team through a crisis.
sentences.business: The astute buyer noticed the contract error and saved ₹2 lakh.
sentences.formal: The astute negotiator always knew when to pause and when to push.
sentences.informal: Astute observation: I notice when the biscuits are finished.

words.usageNote: Astute = quickly and accurately understands complex situations. A top compliment for intelligent, perceptive people.

words.simpleMeaning: of only moderate quality; not very good

sentences.daily: Don't settle for mediocre results when excellence is possible.
sentences.office: The mediocre performance disappointed the entire board.
sentences.interview: Mediocre writing is forgotten; clear writing is remembered.
sentences.business: Mediocre service leads to customer attrition over time.
sentences.formal: Mediocre input leads to mediocre output — every time.
sentences.informal: I am mediocre at cricket. But I play with enthusiasm.

words.usageNote: Mediocre = not terrible, but not good either. It's a slightly negative word for 'just average.' Push past it.

words.simpleMeaning: honest, direct, and open

sentences.daily: I prefer candid feedback to empty praise.
sentences.office: Be candid in your interview — authenticity is valued.
sentences.interview: The candid discussion helped resolve the team conflict.
sentences.business: The CEO was refreshingly candid about the company's struggles.
sentences.formal: A candid self-evaluation helps you grow faster.
sentences.informal: Candid review: 'You speak well in your native language.' Working on English!

words.usageNote: Candid ≠ rude. Candid = honest but respectful. 'Candid conversation' is a professional term for a frank discussion.

words.simpleMeaning: belonging naturally to something; coming from within

sentences.daily: Intrinsic motivation lasts longer than external rewards.
sentences.office: Curiosity is an intrinsic part of a scientist's mindset.
sentences.interview: Intrinsic rewards include pride, purpose, and joy.
sentences.business: The intrinsic value of education cannot be measured in money.
sentences.formal: She found intrinsic satisfaction in teaching underprivileged children.
sentences.informal: My intrinsic motivation for studying: fear of failure.

words.usageNote: Intrinsic = from INSIDE (self-driven). Extrinsic = from OUTSIDE (money, praise). Both motivate, but intrinsic lasts longer.

words.simpleMeaning: possible and practical to do

sentences.daily: Is it feasible to complete this in two days?
sentences.office: A feasibility study was conducted before the project began.
sentences.interview: The proposed solution is technically feasible but costly.
sentences.business: The team assessed whether the plan was feasible given current resources.
sentences.formal: Feasible options were shortlisted and presented to the board.
sentences.informal: Is it feasible to finish this assignment tonight? Technically yes. Realistically no.

words.usageNote: 'Feasible' = can it be done? 'Viable' = is it worth doing? Ask both before committing to any plan.

words.simpleMeaning: capable of working or succeeding

sentences.daily: Is this business model financially viable?
sentences.office: Present only viable solutions, not just wishful thinking.
sentences.interview: A viable alternative to the current system was proposed.
sentences.business: The startup needed a viable revenue model from Day 1.
sentences.formal: Three viable options were evaluated by the steering committee.
sentences.informal: Viable career plan: in progress. Viable eating schedule: also in progress.

words.usageNote: Viable = practical AND sustainable. It must work AND keep working. 'Technically feasible but not commercially viable' is common.

words.simpleMeaning: having mixed feelings about something

sentences.daily: I feel ambivalent about accepting the job offer.
sentences.office: She was ambivalent about leaving her hometown.
sentences.interview: Ambivalent consumers are harder to convert into buyers.
sentences.business: The team was ambivalent about adopting the new software.
sentences.formal: The ambivalent response from clients indicated mixed market interest.
sentences.informal: Ambivalent about dessert: want it, shouldn't have it. Will have it.

words.usageNote: Ambivalent = two opposite feelings at the same time. 'I love this job but also want to quit.' That's ambivalence.

words.simpleMeaning: never done or experienced before

sentences.daily: The pandemic caused unprecedented disruption to the economy.
sentences.office: She achieved unprecedented results in her first quarter.
sentences.interview: The technology offers unprecedented accuracy in diagnosis.
sentences.business: The company posted unprecedented losses during the lockdown.
sentences.formal: The agreement was hailed as an unprecedented diplomatic achievement.
sentences.informal: Unprecedented: waking up before my alarm. Truly historic.

words.usageNote: Unprecedented = the FIRST TIME this has happened. Very strong word. Don't overuse it or it loses its impact.

words.simpleMeaning: similar or comparable to something else

sentences.daily: The situation is analogous to what happened in 2008.
sentences.office: This new policy is analogous to existing data protection laws.
sentences.interview: Using analogous cases strengthens a legal argument.
sentences.business: The product launch strategy was analogous to a successful 2019 campaign.
sentences.formal: Teaching is analogous to planting seeds — results come later.
sentences.informal: My situation is analogous to running late for a train — every morning.

words.usageNote: Analogous = parallel in function or meaning. Used to explain something new by comparing it to something familiar.

words.simpleMeaning: existing from birth; natural and not learned

sentences.daily: She has an innate talent for connecting with people.
sentences.office: Innate curiosity drives the best scientists and engineers.
sentences.interview: Not all skills are innate — most are developed with practice.
sentences.business: The team had an innate ability to find creative solutions.
sentences.formal: His innate leadership ability was visible from the start.
sentences.informal: Innate ability to sleep anywhere: confirmed talent.

words.usageNote: Innate = born with it. But research shows most skills are BUILT, not born. Growth mindset > fixed mindset.

words.simpleMeaning: well-meaning and kind; doing good for others

sentences.daily: The company has a benevolent policy of supporting employees' education.
sentences.office: He was known as a benevolent leader who cared for his people.
sentences.interview: Benevolent organisations give back to the communities they serve.
sentences.business: The foundation's benevolent work reached millions of families.
sentences.formal: Benevolent gestures from leadership build team loyalty.
sentences.informal: Benevolent dictator of the household: me, about chai tea levels.

words.usageNote: Benevolent = genuinely wanting to do good and being kind. Often used in leadership, philanthropy, and descriptions of character.

words.simpleMeaning: real, physical, and measurable

sentences.daily: Show tangible results, not just good intentions.
sentences.office: The training had tangible benefits on team performance.
sentences.interview: We need tangible proof before making the decision.
sentences.business: Tangible improvements were recorded after the new process.
sentences.formal: Tangible assets include land, buildings, and machinery.
sentences.informal: Tangible outcome of study session: 3 pages. Intangible: existential crisis.

words.usageNote: Tangible = physical or clearly measurable. Intangible = abstract, like trust or brand value. Both matter in business.

words.simpleMeaning: the ability to speak or write clearly and expressively

sentences.daily: Her eloquence in English impressed the interview panel.
sentences.office: Eloquence is not about vocabulary — it's about clarity and feeling.
sentences.interview: His eloquence helped him close difficult deals.
sentences.business: The speech was celebrated for its eloquence and passion.
sentences.formal: The advocate's eloquence moved the courtroom to tears.
sentences.informal: Eloquence goal: sound like Shashi Tharoor. Current level: working on it.

words.usageNote: Eloquence comes from practice, preparation, and reading widely. Great speakers read constantly and think in stories.

words.simpleMeaning: the quality of continuing despite difficulty

sentences.daily: Tenacity is what separates those who achieve from those who wish.
sentences.office: She showed tenacity in rebuilding her company after bankruptcy.
sentences.interview: Tenacity and patience are the twin engines of long-term success.
sentences.business: His tenacity in sales led to a record-breaking quarter.
sentences.formal: The team's tenacity through the setbacks was remarkable.
sentences.informal: Tenacity: I've attempted this habit 20 times. I'll try again Monday.

words.usageNote: Tenacity = keeping going when everything says stop. Grit, determination, persistence — all synonyms. All valuable.

words.simpleMeaning: to increase or make something stronger or louder

sentences.daily: Use data to amplify your argument in presentations.
sentences.office: Social media amplifies both truth and misinformation.
sentences.interview: Good leadership amplifies the team's collective strengths.
sentences.business: The campaign amplified the brand's reach by 3×.
sentences.formal: The speaker's words were amplified by the stadium system.
sentences.informal: Coffee amplifies my morning energy and my anxiety equally.

words.simpleMeaning: to combine things to make them stronger or simpler

sentences.daily: Consolidate your notes into one document before the exam.
sentences.office: The company consolidated three departments into one.
sentences.interview: I consolidated my loan payments into a single monthly EMI.
sentences.business: Consolidate your market position before expanding.
sentences.formal: Two regions were consolidated under a single director.
sentences.informal: Consolidating my laundry and my dishes into one catastrophic evening.

words.simpleMeaning: to assess or judge the quality or value of something

sentences.daily: Evaluate each option before making a final decision.
sentences.office: We evaluated the ROI of every marketing channel.
sentences.interview: Teachers evaluate students through exams and assignments.
sentences.business: The committee evaluated 50 proposals and chose five.
sentences.formal: The software was evaluated by the QA team before launch.
sentences.informal: I evaluate movie options for 30 minutes and still pick the wrong one.

words.simpleMeaning: to make a process easier

sentences.daily: Good communication facilitates faster decision-making.
sentences.office: I facilitated the workshop for 30 managers.
sentences.interview: Technology facilitates remote collaboration across time zones.
sentences.business: The mediator facilitated a resolution between both parties.
sentences.formal: The training programme facilitated knowledge transfer efficiently.
sentences.informal: Chai facilitates every productive conversation in India.

words.simpleMeaning: to develop or express something carefully

sentences.daily: Formulate your response before answering in an interview.
sentences.office: The team formulated a three-year digital strategy.
sentences.interview: I formulated a plan to improve customer satisfaction.
sentences.business: The policy was formulated after extensive stakeholder consultation.
sentences.formal: The scientist formulated a new hypothesis based on the data.
sentences.informal: I formulated a plan to exercise daily. Plan formulated. Execution: TBD.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something less severe or harmful

sentences.daily: Invest in training to mitigate skill gaps on the team.
sentences.office: The government took steps to mitigate the impact of inflation.
sentences.interview: Regular testing mitigates software risk significantly.
sentences.business: Insurance is used to mitigate financial risk.
sentences.formal: Safety protocols mitigate the risk of workplace accidents.
sentences.informal: I mitigate traffic stress by leaving early. Or telling myself to leave early.

words.simpleMeaning: to expect and prepare for something before it happens

sentences.daily: Anticipate problems before they become crises.
sentences.office: I anticipated the client's question and prepared an answer.
sentences.interview: We didn't anticipate the level of demand after launch.
sentences.business: Great managers anticipate their team's needs proactively.
sentences.formal: The risk team anticipated regulatory changes and prepared.
sentences.informal: I anticipated hunger and packed two extra rotis. Wise.

words.simpleMeaning: to communicate an idea or feeling

sentences.daily: Convey your ideas clearly and concisely in writing.
sentences.office: His tone of voice conveyed genuine concern.
sentences.interview: How do you convey complex information to a non-technical audience?
sentences.business: The ad conveyed the brand's values in just 30 seconds.
sentences.formal: The report conveys the urgency of the situation effectively.
sentences.informal: I tried to convey frustration calmly. The vein in my neck did not agree.

words.simpleMeaning: to provide evidence to support a claim

sentences.daily: Substantiate every claim in your research paper.
sentences.office: Can you substantiate that number with data?
sentences.interview: The journalist substantiated her claims with recorded evidence.
sentences.business: He couldn't substantiate the complaint with any evidence.
sentences.formal: The audit report substantiated concerns about financial mismanagement.
sentences.informal: I substantiated my request for a raise with 6 months of data.

words.simpleMeaning: to correct an error or wrong

sentences.daily: Rectify the error in the report before submitting.
sentences.office: The team rectified the production issue within 2 hours.
sentences.interview: How will you rectify this mistake with the client?
sentences.business: The software update rectified the critical security vulnerability.
sentences.formal: The billing error was rectified and refunded within a day.
sentences.informal: Rectified my typo in the email — after 10 people had seen it.

words.simpleMeaning: to delay something to a later time; to yield to another's judgment

sentences.daily: We deferred the decision until more data was available.
sentences.office: I defer to the expert on this technical matter.
sentences.interview: The launch was deferred by three weeks due to testing issues.
sentences.business: Defer payment options are offered to financial hardship cases.
sentences.formal: The team deferred final approval to the executive committee.
sentences.informal: I deferred studying until 11 PM. Efficient, as always.

words.simpleMeaning: to confirm something is correct, true, or acceptable

sentences.daily: Validate your assumptions before building the product.
sentences.office: The research validated the initial hypothesis.
sentences.interview: Always validate data before presenting it as fact.
sentences.business: User testing validated our design decisions.
sentences.formal: The certificate was validated by the issuing institution.
sentences.informal: I validated my decision to eat dessert. It was correct.

words.simpleMeaning: to combine parts into a whole

sentences.daily: Integrate all feedback before submitting the final report.
sentences.office: The new employee integrated well with the existing team.
sentences.interview: We integrate data from three sources into one dashboard.
sentences.business: The app integrates with Google Calendar and Outlook.
sentences.formal: Successfully integrating diverse teams requires cultural awareness.
sentences.informal: Integrating sleep and productivity: still trying to crack the code.

words.simpleMeaning: to express something in numbers or amounts

sentences.daily: Quantify your achievements on your resume.
sentences.office: It's hard to quantify the value of employee morale.
sentences.interview: The report quantified the financial impact of the delay.
sentences.business: Can you quantify the improvement in customer satisfaction?
sentences.formal: The risk was quantified at ₹20 lakh per event.
sentences.informal: Quantifying my love of chai: infinite. Units: cups per day.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something easier to understand

sentences.daily: Clarify the scope before the project begins.
sentences.office: I asked the client to clarify their deadline.
sentences.interview: The email clarified the policy changes effectively.
sentences.business: Always clarify ambiguity in instructions before acting.
sentences.formal: The manager clarified expectations to avoid confusion.
sentences.informal: Clarifying question: 'What do you mean by done?' Always worth asking.

words.simpleMeaning: to repeat a process, improving each time

sentences.daily: Great products are built by iterating on user feedback.
sentences.office: We iterated through five design versions before the final.
sentences.interview: Iterate fast — done is better than perfect.
sentences.business: The team iterated the prototype twice before user testing.
sentences.formal: Agile methodology involves iterating in two-week sprints.
sentences.informal: I iterate on my morning routine — still not optimal after 10 years.

words.simpleMeaning: to recognise or understand something with difficulty

sentences.daily: A good leader can discern what truly matters.
sentences.office: I couldn't discern a clear pattern in the data.
sentences.interview: He has a rare ability to discern truth from hype.
sentences.business: Discerning readers notice errors that others miss.
sentences.formal: She discerned the conflict of interest before anyone else.
sentences.informal: It takes skill to discern genuine feedback from flattery.

words.simpleMeaning: to extend conclusions from known data to unknown situations

sentences.daily: Don't extrapolate beyond what the data supports.
sentences.office: Based on Q1 results, we can extrapolate full-year revenue.
sentences.interview: The researcher extrapolated global trends from regional data.
sentences.business: Extrapolating from a small sample can be misleading.
sentences.formal: The model extrapolated future demand using historical growth rates.
sentences.informal: I extrapolated from 2 weeks of habit data that I'll be perfect by July.

words.simpleMeaning: to make things work well together

sentences.daily: Harmonise team goals with company objectives.
sentences.office: The two departments needed to harmonise their processes.
sentences.interview: The policy was harmonised across all regional offices.
sentences.business: Good design harmonises form and function.
sentences.formal: Tax laws were harmonised across the EU member states.
sentences.informal: Harmonising two different music playlists: harder than it sounds.

words.simpleMeaning: to completely renovate or revise something

sentences.daily: We need to overhaul the entire onboarding process.
sentences.office: The team overhauled the app's user interface in Q2.
sentences.interview: A system overhaul was necessary after three security breaches.
sentences.business: The HR policy was completely overhauled to reflect modern practices.
sentences.formal: The car needed a full engine overhaul after 10 years.
sentences.informal: Overhauling my wardrobe: said that 3 years ago.

words.simpleMeaning: to state something confidently and forcefully

sentences.daily: Assert your rights clearly and professionally.
sentences.office: The lawyer asserted that her client was innocent.
sentences.interview: She asserted her position without backing down.
sentences.business: The report asserted a direct link between sleep and productivity.
sentences.formal: He asserted control over the project after the team struggled.
sentences.informal: Asserting my right to the last seat — respectfully.

words.simpleMeaning: to conclude from evidence without being explicitly told

sentences.daily: What can you infer from this graph?
sentences.office: I inferred from his tone that he was unhappy with the result.
sentences.interview: Don't infer more than the data supports.
sentences.business: Good readers infer meaning from context clues.
sentences.formal: The analyst inferred future trends from historical patterns.
sentences.informal: I inferred from the silence that my idea was not great.

words.simpleMeaning: to go beyond the limits of something

sentences.daily: Great leaders transcend their own ego to serve others.
sentences.office: Her talent transcended her lack of formal education.
sentences.interview: Good music transcends language and culture.
sentences.business: The brand transcended local markets to reach a global audience.
sentences.formal: His performance transcended expectations and redefined the role.
sentences.informal: My love of chai transcends all dietary restrictions.

words.hindi: भिन्न होना / अलग दिशा में जाना
words.simpleMeaning: to go in different directions or differ from each other

sentences.daily: Our opinions diverged sharply on the marketing strategy.
sentences.office: The two reports diverged significantly in their conclusions.
sentences.interview: The road diverges into two paths — take the one that scares you.
sentences.business: Audience preferences have diverged across age groups.
sentences.formal: Cultural values diverge even within the same country.
sentences.informal: My plans and reality diverge every single morning.

words.simpleMeaning: to come together toward the same point or conclusion

sentences.daily: The two strategies eventually converged into one plan.
sentences.office: Tech and healthcare are converging to create new innovations.
sentences.interview: All roads converge at the city centre.
sentences.business: The team's diverse ideas converged into a unified proposal.
sentences.formal: Global trends are converging faster than ever before.
sentences.informal: My calendar and my sleep schedule never converge.

words.simpleMeaning: to work with others toward a shared goal

sentences.daily: We collaborated with three agencies on the campaign.
sentences.office: Great things happen when smart people collaborate freely.
sentences.interview: I collaborated with the data team to build the dashboard.
sentences.business: The two companies collaborated to develop a joint product.
sentences.formal: Staff from 10 countries collaborated on the global report.
sentences.informal: Collaborating on where to eat: always the hardest team exercise.

words.simpleMeaning: done on purpose; to think carefully

sentences.daily: This was a deliberate choice, not a mistake.
sentences.office: The committee deliberated for two hours before deciding.
sentences.interview: Deliberate practice is more effective than casual repetition.
sentences.business: The design error was deliberate — it was a test.
sentences.formal: The board deliberated at length on the acquisition strategy.
sentences.informal: Deliberate avoidance of the gym for 3 weeks. Deliberate.

words.simpleMeaning: to bring together for action or effort

sentences.daily: The NGO mobilized volunteers across 12 states.
sentences.office: We mobilized the team in 24 hours to handle the crisis.
sentences.interview: Digital platforms mobilize public opinion at scale.
sentences.business: The startup mobilized ₹2 crore in 48 hours through crowdfunding.
sentences.formal: The government mobilized emergency services within hours.
sentences.informal: Mobilizing my brain before 9 AM: still in development.

words.simpleMeaning: to spread or promote something widely

sentences.daily: Don't propagate misinformation — verify before sharing.
sentences.office: Good practices must be propagated across the organisation.
sentences.interview: Social media propagates ideas faster than any medium before it.
sentences.business: The campaign propagated health awareness across rural communities.
sentences.formal: Knowledge is propagated best through storytelling and examples.
sentences.informal: Propagating good habits: I pin them to the fridge. Results: variable.

words.simpleMeaning: to cancel out or make something invalid

sentences.daily: One bad decision can negate years of good work.
sentences.office: Negativity in the team negated the impact of our training.
sentences.interview: A poor review negates a strong product launch.
sentences.business: The exception clause negated the main contract terms.
sentences.formal: The defense's argument was negated by the video evidence.
sentences.informal: One bad meal negates my entire week of eating well. Apparently.

words.simpleMeaning: to make two things compatible; to resolve a disagreement

sentences.daily: Reconcile your accounts at the end of every month.
sentences.office: The two managers reconciled their differences after mediation.
sentences.interview: It's hard to reconcile quality with speed in this project.
sentences.business: The accounts were reconciled with the bank statements.
sentences.formal: The team was reconciled after the facilitator-led workshop.
sentences.informal: Reconciling what I plan to eat with what I actually eat: daily battle.

words.simpleMeaning: to motivate someone using rewards or benefits

sentences.daily: Incentivize good performance with bonuses and recognition.
sentences.office: The company incentivized early adoption with a 30% discount.
sentences.interview: Customers were incentivized through a referral programme.
sentences.business: Incentivizing the right behaviors drives the right results.
sentences.formal: Tax breaks incentivize investment in green technology.
sentences.informal: Incentivized to work out: bought gym shoes. Still on display.

words.simpleMeaning: to form a concept or idea about something

sentences.daily: Before coding, conceptualise the full user experience.
sentences.office: She conceptualized the product design in one brainstorming session.
sentences.interview: Good innovators conceptualize solutions before they're needed.
sentences.business: The team conceptualized three different campaign directions.
sentences.formal: The architect conceptualized the building's layout before drawing.
sentences.informal: I conceptualized an organised life. Execution: perpetually in progress.

words.simpleMeaning: to copy or reproduce something exactly

sentences.daily: Can you replicate this result in a different market?
sentences.office: The success of the Mumbai branch was replicated in Chennai.
sentences.interview: Scientists replicate experiments to verify results.
sentences.business: This design cannot be replicated without the original files.
sentences.formal: The training was replicated across 40 offices nationwide.
sentences.informal: I replicated my grandmother's recipe — almost. Close enough.

words.simpleMeaning: to transfer something to a different place or context

sentences.daily: Transpose the data from the old format to the new template.
sentences.office: Good ideas can be transposed across industries.
sentences.interview: The melody was transposed to a higher key.
sentences.business: He transposed the successful rural model to an urban context.
sentences.formal: The formula was transposed incorrectly — causing a ₹5 lakh error.
sentences.informal: Transposing habits from holidays to workdays: never easy.

words.simpleMeaning: to set a standard for comparison

sentences.daily: Benchmark your performance against industry standards.
sentences.office: We benchmarked the new process against global best practices.
sentences.interview: HR benchmarked salaries using market data from five surveys.
sentences.business: The analyst benchmarked the startup against 10 comparable firms.
sentences.formal: The service level was benchmarked quarterly for consistency.
sentences.informal: I benchmarked my productivity against my colleague. Big mistake.

words.simpleMeaning: to introduce new ideas or methods

sentences.daily: Innovate or be disrupted — there is no middle ground.
sentences.office: The company constantly innovates to stay ahead.
sentences.interview: We innovated our delivery process to cut costs.
sentences.business: Teams that innovate together build stronger bonds.
sentences.formal: The product team innovated under tight budget constraints.
sentences.informal: I innovated my lunch: old rice + new sauce = new dish!

words.simpleMeaning: to officially require or authorise something

sentences.daily: The policy mandates annual safety training for all staff.
sentences.office: The government mandated masks during the pandemic.
sentences.interview: He was given a mandate to restructure the team.
sentences.business: The board mandated a 20% cost reduction by year-end.
sentences.formal: Attendance was mandated for all department heads.
sentences.informal: Someone mandate a 4-day work week. Please.

words.simpleMeaning: to reduce or prevent the effects of something

sentences.daily: Exercise helps counteract the effects of sedentary work.
sentences.office: The medication counteracts the side effects of the treatment.
sentences.interview: We introduced bonuses to counteract growing staff dissatisfaction.
sentences.business: The ad campaign counteracted negative press coverage.
sentences.formal: Strong governance can counteract the risks of rapid growth.
sentences.informal: I counteract unhealthy eating with… more unhealthy eating.

words.simpleMeaning: to cause something to happen suddenly or earlier than expected

sentences.daily: The poor results precipitated an urgent board review.
sentences.office: The scandal precipitated the CEO's resignation.
sentences.interview: A single tweet precipitated a crisis for the brand.
sentences.business: Lack of planning precipitated the project's failure.
sentences.formal: The news precipitated a sharp drop in share prices.
sentences.informal: My alarm not going off precipitated a very stressful morning.

words.simpleMeaning: to stop trying

sentences.daily: Never give up on learning English — consistency wins.
sentences.office: She wanted to give up, but kept pushing for one more week.
sentences.interview: I refuse to give up on my dream.
sentences.business: The company gave up trying to enter the Chinese market.
sentences.formal: After five failed attempts, he finally gave up the appeal.
sentences.informal: I give up trying to maintain inbox zero. It's impossible.

words.usageNote: 'Give up' + gerund: 'I gave up smoking.' 'Give up on' + noun: 'Don't give up on your goals.'

words.simpleMeaning: to learn or improve; to physically lift

sentences.daily: I picked up Spanish while travelling in South America.
sentences.office: Business is starting to pick up after a slow quarter.
sentences.interview: Can you pick up the report from the printer?
sentences.business: She picked up great interview tips from the workshop.
sentences.formal: Sales picked up significantly after the product relaunch.
sentences.informal: I picked up procrastination in college. Never put it down.

words.usageNote: 'Pick up' a skill = learn naturally. 'Pick up speed' = increase pace. 'Pick up someone' = collect a person.

words.simpleMeaning: to fail mechanically; to explain in steps; to lose emotional control

sentences.daily: Break down the task into smaller steps.
sentences.office: The car broke down on the highway.
sentences.interview: She broke down crying when she heard the news.
sentences.business: The proposal broke down during the final presentation.
sentences.formal: The manager broke down the quarterly data for the team.
sentences.informal: My study plan breaks down every Sunday night. Reliably.

words.usageNote: Three meanings: 1) Stop working (machine). 2) Analyse/simplify. 3) Cry or lose composure. All are common!

words.simpleMeaning: to complete or perform a task or plan

sentences.daily: Carry out the plan we discussed in the meeting.
sentences.office: The team carried out the audit over three days.
sentences.interview: Always carry out a safety check before starting work.
sentences.business: He carried out his responsibilities with exceptional care.
sentences.formal: The research was carried out by an independent team.
sentences.informal: I carried out exactly zero of my weekend plans. Success!

words.usageNote: 'Carry out' = take action to COMPLETE a specific task or plan. More formal than 'do.' Common in reports and instructions.

words.simpleMeaning: to think of or produce a new idea

sentences.daily: Can you come up with three alternatives by tomorrow?
sentences.office: She came up with the best marketing idea of the year.
sentences.interview: I came up with a solution in the middle of the night.
sentences.business: The team came up with a creative workaround.
sentences.formal: No one could come up with a better plan than hers.
sentences.informal: I came up with this excuse. I'm proud of it.

words.usageNote: 'Come up with' = invent or think of something NEW. Always positive and creative in connotation.

words.simpleMeaning: to handle or manage a situation or problem

sentences.daily: I'll deal with the complaint before end of day.
sentences.office: How do you deal with difficult clients?
sentences.interview: Deal with the most urgent issues first.
sentences.business: The team dealt with five crises in one week.
sentences.formal: HR dealt with the workplace dispute professionally.
sentences.informal: Dealing with Mondays: it's a process. Still figuring it out.

words.usageNote: 'Deal with' = take responsibility for handling something. Can mean emotions too: 'I'm still dealing with this.'

words.simpleMeaning: to understand or solve something

sentences.daily: I finally figured out how to use the new software.
sentences.office: Can you figure out the best route to take?
sentences.interview: She figured out the issue in less than 10 minutes.
sentences.business: I couldn't figure out why the formula wasn't working.
sentences.formal: We need to figure out a solution before the meeting.
sentences.informal: I've been trying to figure out my life plan since 2019.

words.usageNote: 'Figure out' = understand something that was unclear, or find a solution to a problem. Very versatile.

words.simpleMeaning: to have a friendly relationship with someone

sentences.daily: I get along well with all my colleagues.
sentences.office: Do you get along with your manager?
sentences.interview: They didn't get along at first but became close friends.
sentences.business: Getting along with clients is essential in customer-facing roles.
sentences.formal: The two departments don't always get along — but they collaborate.
sentences.informal: I get along with everyone. Except my alarm clock.

words.usageNote: 'Get along (with someone)' = have a good, smooth relationship. 'Get along in life' = manage and progress.

words.simpleMeaning: to wait; to keep going despite difficulty

sentences.daily: Hold on — I'll be with you in a minute.
sentences.office: Hold on to your dreams even when it's hard.
sentences.interview: Can you hold on while I check the details?
sentences.business: Hold on — something is wrong with this data.
sentences.formal: The startup held on through three difficult years.
sentences.informal: Hold on — I need to check if this is a good idea. Spoiler: it is.

words.usageNote: 'Hold on' = wait (informal). 'Hold on to' = keep/retain something. 'Hold on through' = endure a tough period.

words.simpleMeaning: to feel excited and positive about a future event

sentences.daily: I look forward to meeting you on Tuesday.
sentences.office: I'm looking forward to starting my new job.
sentences.interview: She looked forward to every training session.
sentences.business: The team looked forward to the annual retreat.
sentences.formal: I look forward to your feedback on this proposal.
sentences.informal: I look forward to Fridays the way I look forward to nothing else.

words.usageNote: Always followed by a noun or gerund (-ing form): 'I look forward to hearing from you' — NOT 'look forward to hear.'

words.simpleMeaning: to invent; to resolve a conflict; cosmetics

sentences.daily: Don't make up excuses — tell the truth.
sentences.office: They fought and then made up quickly.
sentences.interview: I made up a story to explain the delay.
sentences.business: The team made up after the project conflict was resolved.
sentences.formal: She made up the entire presentation on the spot.
sentences.informal: I make up great plans. The follow-through is the issue.

words.usageNote: 'Make up a story' = invent. 'Make up (with someone)' = reconcile. 'Put on makeup' = cosmetics. Three meanings!

words.simpleMeaning: to draw attention to something

sentences.daily: Can you point out the error in this report?
