sentences.office: She pointed out a key risk we had overlooked.
sentences.interview: Please point out any mistakes in my work.
sentences.business: The analyst pointed out three gaps in the data.
sentences.formal: He pointed out that the deadline had already passed.
sentences.informal: Pointing out errors in other people's work: an art form.

words.usageNote: 'Point out' = bring something specific to someone's attention. Constructive and professional. Different from 'criticise.'

words.simpleMeaning: to delay or postpone; to make someone lose interest

sentences.daily: Stop putting off your important tasks until evening.
sentences.office: The meeting was put off until next Thursday.
sentences.interview: His rude tone put off the entire team.
sentences.business: I keep putting off updating my LinkedIn profile.
sentences.formal: The deadline was put off by a week due to client changes.
sentences.informal: I keep putting off 'beginning' — and the irony is not lost on me.

words.usageNote: 'Put off a task' = delay. 'Put someone off' = make them lose interest or enthusiasm. Two different uses!

words.simpleMeaning: to use all of something so none is left

sentences.daily: We've run out of time for this discussion.
sentences.office: I ran out of internet data two days before the recharge.
sentences.interview: The team ran out of ideas after two hours.
sentences.business: We ran out of stock during the sale campaign.
sentences.formal: The patience of the committee ran out after the third delay.
sentences.informal: Ran out of coffee. Day cancelled.

words.usageNote: 'Run out of' something = have NONE left. 'Running low on' = almost finished. Two useful phrases.

words.simpleMeaning: to appear or arrive; to be present

sentences.daily: Showing up consistently is half the battle.
sentences.office: She showed up late but delivered perfectly.
sentences.interview: Just show up — presence matters more than perfection.
sentences.business: Three team members didn't show up for the meeting.
sentences.formal: The vendor showed up an hour early for installation.
sentences.informal: I show up for every meeting. The enthusiasm varies.

words.usageNote: 'Show up' (arrive/appear) is more informal than 'attend.' In life: 'Just show up' = presence and consistency matter.

words.hindi: प्रतिनिधित्व करना / किसी बात के लिए खड़ा होना
words.simpleMeaning: to represent; to tolerate

sentences.daily: What does your brand stand for?
sentences.office: I won't stand for dishonesty in any form.
sentences.interview: The abbreviation 'ETA' stands for 'Estimated Time of Arrival'.
sentences.business: Our company stands for quality, integrity, and care.
sentences.formal: She stood for fairness even when it was unpopular.
sentences.informal: My username stands for my initials and birth year. Very creative.

words.usageNote: 'Stand for something' = what it represents or means. 'Won't stand for' = refuse to tolerate.

words.simpleMeaning: to look after; to handle

sentences.daily: I'll take care of the invoicing while you handle the client.
sentences.office: Take care of your health — work can wait.
sentences.interview: She takes care of all the admin for the department.
sentences.business: The team took care of every detail for the launch event.
sentences.formal: He took care of customer complaints with grace and speed.
sentences.informal: I'll take care of dinner. (Orders food online immediately.)

words.usageNote: 'Take care of' = handle a task OR care for a person. Both are essential life phrases.

words.simpleMeaning: to reject an offer; to reduce volume or intensity

sentences.daily: I turned down the offer because the salary was too low.
sentences.office: Turn down the music — I'm on a call!
sentences.interview: Never turn down a chance to learn.
sentences.business: The board turned down the acquisition proposal.
sentences.formal: She turned down the partnership without giving a reason.
sentences.informal: I turned down the alarm. Just five more minutes. (× 10)

words.usageNote: 'Turn down an offer' = decline. 'Turn down the volume' = reduce. Two separate meanings — context is everything.

words.simpleMeaning: to find a solution; to exercise; to end well

sentences.daily: It'll work out — just be patient.
sentences.office: Let's work out a schedule that suits everyone.
sentences.interview: I work out every morning to stay focused.
sentences.business: The team worked out the technical issues overnight.
sentences.formal: Eventually, the partnership worked out for both companies.
sentences.informal: It'll all work out. I tell myself this daily. Occasionally it's true.

words.usageNote: Three meanings: 1) Solve/figure out. 2) Exercise. 3) End well/successfully. All three are very commonly used.

words.simpleMeaning: to increase or develop gradually

sentences.daily: Build up your vocabulary by learning 10 words daily.
sentences.office: She built up her business from scratch over 5 years.
sentences.interview: Build up trust before asking for something big.
sentences.business: The team built up momentum over the first quarter.
sentences.formal: A slow buildup of customer complaints eventually became a crisis.
sentences.informal: Building up my study habit — still in progress after 6 months.

words.usageNote: 'Build up' = develop or increase gradually over time. Very useful for skills, habits, and business growth.

words.simpleMeaning: to reach the same level; to update each other

sentences.daily: I need to catch up on emails from last week.
sentences.office: Let's catch up over coffee next week.
sentences.interview: He caught up on the missed lectures using recordings.
sentences.business: She caught up with the team after her leave.
sentences.formal: The company caught up to its competitor in market share.
sentences.informal: Caught up on three weeks of chores in one day. New record.

words.usageNote: 'Catch up on' = complete things you missed. 'Catch up with' = reconnect with someone. Both are essential.

words.simpleMeaning: to reduce or decrease something

sentences.daily: We need to cut back on unnecessary expenses.
sentences.office: The company cut back 20% of its workforce.
sentences.interview: I'm cutting back on caffeine this month.
sentences.business: Advertising costs were cut back during the slow season.
sentences.formal: The project scope was cut back to meet the deadline.
sentences.informal: Cutting back on screen time: watch 5 shows instead of 6.

words.usageNote: 'Cut back on' something = reduce the amount or frequency. Important in both personal finance and business.

words.simpleMeaning: to prepare or write a formal document

sentences.daily: Draw up a detailed plan before the client presentation.
sentences.office: The lawyer drew up the contract in two days.
sentences.interview: I'll draw up a list of requirements by end of day.
sentences.business: An agreement was drawn up and signed by all parties.
sentences.formal: The proposal was drawn up over a weekend.
sentences.informal: I drew up a budget. Then ignored it immediately.

words.usageNote: 'Draw up a contract/document/plan' = to prepare and write something formally. Very common in legal and business contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to be slower than expected; to not make progress

sentences.daily: I fell behind on my reading during busy weeks.
sentences.office: The project fell behind schedule by two weeks.
sentences.interview: Don't fall behind — consistent effort beats cramming.
sentences.business: The country fell behind in renewable energy adoption.
sentences.formal: She fell behind on rent and needed a payment plan.
sentences.informal: I fell behind on my habit tracker. To track my progress. Ironic.

words.usageNote: 'Fall behind (on something)' = not keeping up. 'Falling behind schedule' is extremely common in project management.

words.simpleMeaning: to complete a form; to inform someone of missed info

sentences.daily: Fill in the application form carefully.
sentences.office: Can you fill me in on what I missed in the meeting?
sentences.interview: She filled in for the team lead during his leave.
sentences.business: Fill in all mandatory fields before submitting the form.
sentences.formal: The new intern was filled in on the project background.
sentences.informal: Filled in my diary: three entries since January. Great habit.

words.usageNote: 'Fill in a form' = complete it. 'Fill someone in' = update them. 'Fill in for someone' = replace them temporarily.

words.simpleMeaning: to stay at the same level or pace as something

sentences.daily: It's hard to keep up with all the new AI developments.
sentences.office: She keeps up with industry trends through daily reading.
sentences.interview: I struggle to keep up with the workload some weeks.
sentences.business: The company kept up with competitor pricing by adjusting quarterly.
sentences.formal: Keeping up with technology is a lifelong commitment.
sentences.informal: Can't keep up with the news. It changes every hour.

words.usageNote: 'Keep up with' = maintain pace with something or someone. 'Keep up' on its own = continue, don't stop.

words.simpleMeaning: to disappoint someone

sentences.daily: I never want to let my team down.
sentences.office: He let the client down by missing the deadline.
sentences.interview: Don't let yourself down by not preparing.
sentences.business: The product let customers down with poor quality.
sentences.formal: She felt let down when her promotion was given to someone else.
sentences.informal: I let myself down by not studying. Regular occurrence.

words.usageNote: 'Let someone down' = disappoint them by failing to meet their expectations. Powerful emotionally.

words.simpleMeaning: to progress; to stop focusing on the past

sentences.daily: Move on from failure — learn and keep going.
sentences.office: Let's move on to the next agenda item.
sentences.interview: She moved on from the difficult experience with grace.
sentences.business: The team moved on to the implementation phase.
sentences.formal: After the rejection, I moved on and applied to 10 more companies.
sentences.informal: Moving on from that embarrassing moment. Or trying to.

words.usageNote: 'Move on' = stop dwelling on something and progress forward. Also used in meetings to change topics.

words.simpleMeaning: to make contact with someone

sentences.daily: Reach out if you have any questions.
sentences.office: I reached out to three mentors for career advice.
sentences.interview: The company reaches out to customers with personalised emails.
sentences.business: She reached out to reconnect after five years.
sentences.formal: Feel free to reach out anytime — I'm here to help.
sentences.informal: Reached out to an old friend. Took 3 weeks to reply. Worth it.

words.usageNote: 'Reach out to someone' = initiate contact. Very common in professional emails and LinkedIn messages.

words.simpleMeaning: to prepare or establish something

sentences.daily: Can you set up a meeting for next Monday?
sentences.office: She set up her own business at 26.
sentences.interview: Set up your workspace before you begin working.
sentences.business: The team set up the product demo environment overnight.
sentences.formal: The new office was set up and ready in three days.
sentences.informal: Set up a new habit tracker app. Day 1 done. Day 2: forgot.

words.usageNote: 'Set up a meeting/system/business' = establish or prepare it. One of the most commonly used phrasal verbs.

words.simpleMeaning: to take on more responsibility; to improve

sentences.daily: We need everyone to step up this quarter.
sentences.office: She stepped up as team lead when the manager was on leave.
sentences.interview: The brand stepped up its quality standards significantly.
sentences.business: Step up your communication skills to advance in your career.
sentences.formal: The team stepped up production to meet the surge in demand.
sentences.informal: Stepped up my study game. Slightly. Progress!

words.usageNote: 'Step up' = take on more responsibility or do better. A call to action in leadership: 'Step up and lead.'

words.simpleMeaning: to take control of something

sentences.daily: Can you take over the project while I'm on leave?
sentences.office: The new manager took over the team smoothly.
sentences.interview: The company was taken over by a larger corporation.
sentences.business: I'll take over the presentation from slide 10.
sentences.formal: Automation is taking over many repetitive tasks.
sentences.informal: Took over the kitchen. Disaster followed. Then pizza was ordered.

words.usageNote: 'Take over a task' = continue someone else's work. 'Take over a company' = acquire it. Both are common.

words.simpleMeaning: to consider every aspect of something carefully

sentences.daily: Think through every risk before committing.
sentences.office: She thought through all options before making the decision.
sentences.interview: Think the plan through before presenting it to the board.
sentences.business: The engineer thought through the architecture thoroughly.
sentences.formal: The CEO thought through the acquisition for six months.
sentences.informal: I thought through my morning routine once. Still chaotic.

words.usageNote: 'Think through' = think carefully about ALL aspects, not just the obvious ones. Essential before major decisions.

words.simpleMeaning: to end up in a situation; to finish something

sentences.daily: If you don't plan, you wind up reacting to everything.
sentences.office: We wound up staying at the office until 9 PM.
sentences.interview: The meeting wound up much later than expected.
sentences.business: The startup wound up closing after running out of funding.
sentences.formal: She wound up choosing the riskiest option — and it paid off.
sentences.informal: I wound up binge-watching instead of studying. Classic.

words.usageNote: 'Wind up' = end up in a situation, often unplanned. 'Wind up a meeting' = bring it to a close.

words.simpleMeaning: to review or check something carefully

sentences.daily: Go over the report before the meeting starts.
sentences.office: Let me go over the key points one more time.
sentences.interview: The accountant went over every expense in the budget.
sentences.business: Please go over the contract before signing anything.
sentences.formal: The manager went over the presentation with the team.
sentences.informal: Go over my plan: sounds great in theory, falls apart in practice.

words.usageNote: 'Go over' something = review it carefully. Very useful before meetings, exams, interviews, and submissions.

words.simpleMeaning: to mention a topic; to raise a child

sentences.daily: Please bring up this issue in today's meeting.
sentences.office: I was brought up to always say thank you.
sentences.interview: She bravely brought up the salary disparity issue.
sentences.business: He brought up a valid point about the project timeline.
sentences.formal: The audit team brought up concerns about the approval process.
sentences.informal: Brought up that I deserved a raise. Brought up again. And again.

words.usageNote: 'Bring up a topic' = introduce it for discussion. 'Bring up a child' = raise and care for a child. Two meanings.

words.simpleMeaning: to cancel something

sentences.daily: The outdoor event was called off due to heavy rain.
sentences.office: Let's call off the meeting — the key stakeholder is unavailable.
sentences.interview: The deal was called off after the due diligence findings.
sentences.business: The strike was called off after the management agreed to demands.
sentences.formal: The pitch was called off when the investor pulled out.
sentences.informal: Called off plans to exercise. Weather was involved. Always weather.

words.usageNote: 'Call off' = cancel something that was already scheduled. Different from 'cancel' only in register — both mean the same.

words.simpleMeaning: to continue doing something

sentences.daily: Carry on with the work — I'll join in 5 minutes.
sentences.office: She carried on despite many setbacks.
sentences.interview: Carry on learning English every day — it compounds.
sentences.business: The team carried on after the manager resigned.
sentences.formal: Please carry on with the presentation.
sentences.informal: Carry on with studying. (Immediately distracted by phone.)

words.usageNote: 'Carry on' = keep going; continue. 'Carry on doing something' = don't stop. British English prefers 'carry on.'

words.simpleMeaning: to interrupt; to stop supply; to isolate

sentences.daily: Don't cut me off — let me finish my point.
sentences.office: The internet was cut off during the storm.
sentences.interview: He was cut off from his team during the crisis.
sentences.business: The funding was cut off without warning.
sentences.formal: The speaker was cut off before reaching his conclusion.
sentences.informal: Cut off by a car on the highway. Daily experience.

words.usageNote: 'Cut someone off' (in conversation) = interrupt. 'Cut off supply/funding' = stop it. 'Cut off a route' = block it.

words.simpleMeaning: to use knowledge or experience as a resource

sentences.daily: Draw on your past experience when solving new problems.
sentences.office: She drew on her teaching background to train the team.
sentences.interview: The speaker drew on 20 years of industry insight.
sentences.business: The report draws on data from 15 countries.
sentences.formal: Draw on every resource available before making the decision.
sentences.informal: Drawing on my experience of failing exams to teach others better.

words.usageNote: 'Draw on something' = use it as a resource or source of support. Common in interviews: 'I draw on my experience of...'

words.simpleMeaning: to reduce pressure or intensity

sentences.daily: Ease up on the deadlines — the team is overwhelmed.
sentences.office: Traffic usually eases up after 10 AM.
sentences.interview: The pressure eased up after the launch was complete.
sentences.business: She eased up on her diet after reaching her goal weight.
sentences.formal: The boss finally eased up after three months of high pressure.
sentences.informal: Ease up on the self-criticism — you're doing fine!

words.usageNote: 'Ease up' = relax pressure or effort. 'Ease up on someone' = be less strict or demanding with them.

words.simpleMeaning: to complete what you started

sentences.daily: Follow through on your commitments — every time.
sentences.office: She followed through on every promise she made.
sentences.interview: Following through is what separates good from great.
sentences.business: The team followed through on the pilot programme successfully.
sentences.formal: He didn't follow through, and the client was unhappy.
sentences.informal: I follow through on plans sometimes. It's improving.

words.usageNote: 'Follow through on a commitment/promise/plan' = actually DO what you said. The most important professional habit.

words.simpleMeaning: to prepare for something important

sentences.daily: Gear up for the big presentation tomorrow.
sentences.office: The team is gearing up for the product launch.
sentences.interview: Companies gear up for the festive season months in advance.
sentences.business: I geared up for the interview with three mock sessions.
sentences.formal: The factory geared up production to handle peak demand.
sentences.informal: Gearing up for Monday: on Sunday night, always.

words.usageNote: 'Gear up for something' = actively prepare and get ready for an upcoming event or challenge.

words.simpleMeaning: to stop from moving forward; to restrain

sentences.daily: Don't hold back — share your honest opinion.
sentences.office: Fear is what holds most people back from success.
sentences.interview: He held back his frustration professionally.
sentences.business: Bureaucracy often holds back innovation in large companies.
sentences.formal: She was held back by self-doubt for years.
sentences.informal: What's holding me back? Me. Definitely me.

words.usageNote: 'Hold back' = prevent progress (something holds you back) OR suppress a feeling (hold back emotion).

words.simpleMeaning: to start something, often enthusiastically

sentences.daily: Let's kick off the meeting with a quick update.
sentences.office: The project kicks off on the first of next month.
sentences.interview: They kicked off the campaign with a live event.
sentences.business: The event kicked off with a motivational keynote.
sentences.formal: The sprint kicked off with goal-setting for the team.
sentences.informal: Kicked off my diet on Monday. And Tuesday. And Wednesday.

words.usageNote: 'Kick off' = begin with energy and intention. 'The kick-off meeting' is the first meeting of a project. Very common.

words.simpleMeaning: to present or arrange something clearly

sentences.daily: Lay out your argument clearly before presenting.
sentences.office: She laid out the project plan on a single slide.
sentences.interview: Lay out all options before making a decision.
sentences.business: The architect laid out the design in great detail.
sentences.formal: The proposal clearly laid out the timeline and budget.
sentences.informal: Laid out my study plan. Beautiful. Followed it: not at all.

words.usageNote: 'Lay out a plan/argument/design' = present it in an organised, clear way. Very common in business writing.

words.simpleMeaning: to reduce choices to a smaller number

sentences.daily: Narrow down your options to three before deciding.
sentences.office: We narrowed down the candidates to a final shortlist of 5.
sentences.interview: The investigation narrowed down the cause to one department.
sentences.business: Narrow down the scope before the project begins.
sentences.formal: She narrowed down her career options to two industries.
sentences.informal: Narrowing down where to eat: from 20 options to 18.

words.usageNote: 'Narrow down' = reduce from many to fewer. Common in hiring, research, decisions, and strategy.

words.simpleMeaning: to become more open or share more; to begin operations

sentences.daily: Open up about your challenges — don't bottle things up.
sentences.office: The new market opened up after the regulation changed.
sentences.interview: She opened up about her struggles in the interview.
sentences.business: The platform opened up a new revenue stream.
sentences.formal: The company opened up offices in three new cities.
sentences.informal: I opened up once. It was terrifying. Slowly getting better.

words.usageNote: 'Open up to someone' = share personal feelings honestly. 'Open up a market' = enter it. Two separate uses.

words.simpleMeaning: to yield results; to repay a debt

sentences.daily: Your hard work will pay off — just be consistent.
sentences.office: The investment paid off within 18 months.
sentences.interview: I paid off my loan two years early.
sentences.business: Taking the extra course finally paid off during the promotion round.
sentences.formal: The company's R&D investment paid off with a breakthrough product.
sentences.informal: English practice paying off: you reading this!

words.usageNote: 'Pay off' (effort) = produce positive results. 'Pay off debt' = finish repaying a loan. Both are common.

words.simpleMeaning: to increase in size or production

sentences.daily: We plan to scale up operations next year.
sentences.office: The pilot programme was scaled up nationally after success.
sentences.interview: Scale up production to meet the holiday season demand.
sentences.business: The company scaled up hiring by 40% in Q3.
sentences.formal: Technology helped the startup scale up efficiently.
sentences.informal: Plan: scale up productivity. Reality: steady as she goes.

words.simpleMeaning: a step-by-step set of instructions for solving a problem

sentences.daily: Search engines use algorithms to rank web pages.
sentences.office: The recommendation algorithm shows you what you like.
sentences.interview: An algorithm is a recipe — follow the steps, get the result.
sentences.business: The trading algorithm analysed 10,000 data points per second.
sentences.formal: The algorithm was refined after user feedback.
sentences.informal: My morning algorithm: alarm, phone, 20 minutes of nothing.

words.usageNote: Algorithm = a set of rules for a computer (or human) to follow. Used in coding, AI, and data analysis.

words.simpleMeaning: using technology to perform tasks without human involvement

sentences.daily: Automation is changing every industry in the world.
sentences.office: Email automation saved our team 5 hours per week.
sentences.interview: Automation handles repetitive tasks so humans can focus creatively.
sentences.business: Factory automation increased production by 60%.
sentences.formal: Marketing automation triggered emails based on user behaviour.
sentences.informal: Automation took over my calendar — I just confirm meetings now.

words.usageNote: Automation = machines or software doing work previously done by humans. Can eliminate some jobs, create others.

words.simpleMeaning: the rate at which data is transferred over a network

sentences.daily: High bandwidth allows smooth video streaming.
sentences.office: The server's bandwidth was maxed out during the product launch.
sentences.interview: Upgrade your internet bandwidth for video conferencing.
sentences.business: Low bandwidth causes video calls to freeze and stutter.
sentences.formal: The app uses minimal bandwidth for users in low-connectivity areas.
sentences.informal: Bandwidth fine. My phone's battery: not so fine.

words.usageNote: Tech: bandwidth = data transmission speed. Work context: 'I don't have bandwidth' = no capacity. Same word, two uses.

words.simpleMeaning: storing and accessing data over the internet, not locally

sentences.daily: Cloud computing lets you access files from anywhere.
sentences.office: Our company migrated to cloud computing to reduce IT costs.
sentences.interview: Google Drive and Dropbox are cloud computing services.
sentences.business: Cloud computing enables real-time collaboration across time zones.
sentences.formal: The startup reduced IT costs by 40% through cloud computing.
sentences.informal: Everything I own is in the cloud. And yet I'm still disorganised.

words.usageNote: Cloud computing = infrastructure you rent over the internet. AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure are the big three.

words.simpleMeaning: the practice of protecting systems from digital attacks

sentences.daily: Cybersecurity is a top priority for every modern organisation.
sentences.office: Strong passwords are the first line of cybersecurity.
sentences.interview: Cybersecurity threats have increased significantly post-pandemic.
sentences.business: The company hired a cybersecurity team after the data breach.
sentences.formal: Employee training is critical to effective cybersecurity.
sentences.informal: Cybersecurity advice: don't use 'password123'. I know. I know.

words.usageNote: Cybersecurity = protecting digital systems from hackers, viruses, and unauthorised access. A fast-growing career field.

words.simpleMeaning: converting data into a code to prevent unauthorised access

sentences.daily: End-to-end encryption protects your messages from being read.
sentences.office: WhatsApp uses encryption to secure user conversations.
sentences.interview: All financial data is stored with 256-bit encryption.
sentences.business: Without encryption, sensitive data is vulnerable to theft.
sentences.formal: The app uses encryption to protect your health records.
sentences.informal: Encryption: so my embarrassing messages remain private. Thank you, WhatsApp.

words.usageNote: Encryption = scrambling data so only authorised parties can read it. Essential for banking, health, and communications.

words.simpleMeaning: a way for software systems to communicate

sentences.daily: The weather app uses an API to fetch real-time data.
sentences.office: Developers use APIs to connect different software systems.
sentences.interview: We integrated payment via a payment gateway API.
sentences.business: The map was embedded using Google Maps API.
sentences.formal: Our platform offers open APIs for third-party developers.
sentences.informal: API: how apps talk to each other. Like WhatsApp calling your bank.

words.usageNote: API = Application Programming Interface. It lets different apps share data and functions without exposing their code.

words.simpleMeaning: computer systems performing tasks that require human intelligence

sentences.daily: Artificial intelligence is transforming healthcare, education, and business.
sentences.office: AI-powered tools can write, design, and code.
sentences.interview: We use AI to personalise the user experience.
sentences.business: Artificial intelligence models learn from vast amounts of data.
sentences.formal: The company invested heavily in artificial intelligence research.
sentences.informal: AI: writing my assignment? Helping me understand? Both? Absolutely both.

words.usageNote: AI = computer systems that simulate human thinking. Machine learning is how AI learns. Deep learning is a subset of ML.

words.simpleMeaning: AI technology that learns from data without being programmed

sentences.daily: Netflix uses machine learning to recommend content.
sentences.office: Machine learning models improve as they process more data.
sentences.interview: I'm studying machine learning to build smarter applications.
sentences.business: Machine learning helps detect fraud in banking transactions.
sentences.formal: The demand forecasting tool was powered by machine learning.
sentences.informal: Machine learning: teaching computers to do what I couldn't in school.

words.usageNote: Machine learning ≠ AI. ML is a SUBSET of AI. The model trains on data and improves over time automatically.

words.simpleMeaning: extremely large datasets that require special tools to analyse

sentences.daily: Big data helps businesses understand customer patterns.
sentences.office: The healthcare industry uses big data to predict outbreaks.
sentences.interview: We analyse big data to improve campaign performance.
sentences.business: Big data processing requires specialised infrastructure and tools.
sentences.formal: The company built a big data pipeline to ingest real-time sensor data.
sentences.informal: My search history is basically big data for my overthinking.

words.usageNote: Big data = too large for Excel. Requires tools like Hadoop, Spark, or BigQuery. Volume, Velocity, Variety = the 3 Vs.

words.simpleMeaning: a secure, decentralised digital ledger technology

sentences.daily: Blockchain powers Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies.
sentences.office: Blockchain ensures transactions are transparent and tamper-proof.
sentences.interview: Supply chain transparency is improved with blockchain.
sentences.business: The contract was executed as a smart contract on blockchain.
sentences.formal: Blockchain technology eliminates the need for a trusted middleman.
sentences.informal: Blockchain: I understand 40% of it and I'm okay with that.

words.usageNote: Blockchain = a chain of digital records shared across many computers. No single person controls it. Very secure.

words.simpleMeaning: how a user feels when interacting with a product

sentences.daily: Good UX makes apps feel natural and effortless.
sentences.office: The redesign improved UX by reducing steps from 7 to 3.
sentences.interview: Always design with the user in mind — that's UX thinking.
sentences.business: The app's UX was rated 4.9/5 by beta users.
sentences.formal: UX and UI work together — experience and interface.
sentences.informal: Using an app with terrible UX: please, just let me pay you easily!

words.usageNote: UX = how it FEELS to use something. UI = how it LOOKS. Great products need both. 'User-centred design' = UX principle.

words.simpleMeaning: the visual elements a user interacts with in a product

sentences.daily: The new UI used clean fonts and minimal colour.
sentences.office: The UI was redesigned after poor usability test results.
sentences.interview: UI design includes buttons, icons, typography, and layout.
sentences.business: The app's UI was praised for its simplicity and elegance.
sentences.formal: UI improvements led to a 30% increase in user retention.
sentences.informal: When UI is bad: I spend 5 minutes looking for the 'logout' button.

words.usageNote: UI = what you SEE and CLICK. UX = what you FEEL. Both are measured in user testing.

words.simpleMeaning: software delivered via the internet on a subscription basis

sentences.daily: Zoom, Slack, and Notion are popular SaaS products.
sentences.office: SaaS allows businesses to use software without maintaining it.
sentences.interview: The company generates 80% of revenue from SaaS subscriptions.
sentences.business: SaaS products require internet access to function.
sentences.formal: We migrated from installed software to a SaaS model in Q2.
sentences.informal: SaaS: software you never own but always pay for. Worth it if it works.

words.usageNote: SaaS = Software as a Service. You access it via the internet and pay monthly. No installation needed. Example: Gmail.

words.simpleMeaning: a flexible project management approach using short cycles

sentences.daily: We work in agile sprints of two weeks.
sentences.office: Agile methodology allows teams to adapt quickly to changes.
sentences.interview: I transitioned from waterfall to agile and never looked back.
sentences.business: The product team runs agile standups every morning.
sentences.formal: Agile projects deliver working software in small, fast iterations.
sentences.informal: Agile: fast, flexible, and frequent meetings. Very frequent.

words.usageNote: Agile = deliver in small working chunks, get feedback, improve. Key terms: sprint, backlog, scrum, standup, retrospective.

words.simpleMeaning: a short, time-boxed period of focused work in agile

sentences.daily: We completed the feature in the 2-week sprint.
sentences.office: Sprint planning sets goals for the next iteration.
sentences.interview: The development sprint focused on payment integration.
sentences.business: Sprint velocity measures how much work a team can complete.
sentences.formal: Each sprint ends with a review and retrospective.
sentences.informal: This sprint's goal: write 500 lines of code. Actual output: 200. Improving!

words.usageNote: Sprint = 1–4 weeks of focused work on specific goals. Ends with a review (what was built) and retrospective (what to improve).

words.simpleMeaning: an agile framework for developing and delivering complex products

sentences.daily: The development team uses Scrum as their framework.
sentences.office: Daily Scrum meetings are called standups.
sentences.interview: The Scrum master facilitates the process and removes blockers.
sentences.business: Our product backlog is managed using the Scrum framework.
sentences.formal: Scrum ceremonies include sprint planning, review, and retrospective.
sentences.informal: Daily scrum at 9 AM: 15 minutes, three questions. Simple but effective.

words.usageNote: Scrum roles: Product Owner (what to build), Scrum Master (how to work), Development Team (who builds). Learn all three.

words.simpleMeaning: combining development and operations for faster software delivery

sentences.daily: DevOps practices reduced our deployment time by 80%.
sentences.office: DevOps bridges the gap between development and IT operations.
sentences.interview: The DevOps team automates testing and deployment pipelines.
sentences.business: DevOps culture emphasises collaboration, not blame.
sentences.formal: We adopted DevOps and now deploy daily instead of monthly.
sentences.informal: DevOps: developers and ops people agreeing. Rare and beautiful.

words.usageNote: DevOps = Development + Operations working together. CI/CD pipelines automate testing and deployment for faster delivery.

words.simpleMeaning: a storage location for code or data

sentences.daily: All code is stored in the GitHub repository.
sentences.office: Clone the repository and follow the setup instructions.
sentences.interview: The data repository contains 10 years of customer records.
sentences.business: Each project has its own version-controlled repository.
sentences.formal: The repository was made public after the product launch.
sentences.informal: My GitHub repository: full of unfinished projects and good intentions.

words.usageNote: Repository (repo) = where code is stored and managed. GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket are popular platforms.

words.simpleMeaning: the process of finding and fixing errors in code

sentences.daily: Debugging can take longer than writing the original code.
sentences.office: I spent 3 hours debugging a missing semicolon.
sentences.interview: Good debugging skills are essential for every developer.
sentences.business: The QA team found 12 bugs during the debugging phase.
sentences.formal: Debugging tools like breakpoints help isolate issues quickly.
sentences.informal: Debugging: 10% fixing the issue. 90% finding the issue.

words.usageNote: Debugging = finding and removing bugs (errors) in code. Print statements, debuggers, and rubber ducks are common tools.

words.simpleMeaning: a new version or repetition of a process

sentences.daily: Each iteration improved the design based on feedback.
sentences.office: We're on the third iteration of the product roadmap.
sentences.interview: Iteration is faster than trying to get it perfect the first time.
sentences.business: The algorithm was refined across 10 iterations.
sentences.formal: The team shipped 4 iterations of the feature in one month.
sentences.informal: Iteration 1: rough. Iteration 2: better. Iteration 10: finally decent.

words.usageNote: Iteration = one pass through a process. Iterative design = keep improving through cycles. 'Done is better than perfect.'

words.simpleMeaning: the ability of a system to handle growth

sentences.daily: Scalability is essential when building a product for millions.
sentences.office: The database lacked scalability and crashed under high traffic.
sentences.interview: Cloud systems are built for scalability from Day 1.
sentences.business: The architecture was redesigned for better scalability.
sentences.formal: Scalability is a top concern for any startup expecting rapid growth.
sentences.informal: Scalability check: one user? Works. Ten thousand users? Also works. Success.

words.usageNote: Scalability = can the system GROW without breaking? 'It scales well' = it can handle increasing load.

words.simpleMeaning: software with publicly available code anyone can use

sentences.daily: Linux is one of the most successful open source projects.
sentences.office: Open source software allows anyone to use, modify, and share code.
sentences.interview: We built our product on open source frameworks to save costs.
sentences.business: The company contributed to open source to attract developer talent.
sentences.formal: Open source tools like VSCode and Git are used globally.
sentences.informal: Using open source tools for free. Donating back? Aspirational.

words.usageNote: Open source ≠ free (as in money). It's free to use and modify the code. Companies still make money from services.

words.simpleMeaning: the delay between a request and a response

sentences.daily: High latency causes lag in video calls.
sentences.office: Reducing latency improves user experience significantly.
sentences.interview: The server latency was reduced from 500ms to 50ms.
sentences.business: Low latency is critical for real-time gaming and trading.
sentences.formal: Edge computing reduces latency by processing data closer to users.
sentences.informal: Meeting latency: my brain's delay in understanding complex slides.

words.usageNote: Latency = how LONG it takes to get a response. Bandwidth = how MUCH data. Both affect performance.

words.simpleMeaning: an early model of a product for testing

sentences.daily: We built a clickable prototype before writing a single line of code.
sentences.office: The prototype was tested by 50 users in the first week.
sentences.interview: Never skip the prototype stage — it saves massive rework.
sentences.business: The prototype revealed three critical design flaws.
sentences.formal: The product prototype was presented to investors at the pitch.
sentences.informal: Prototype: it does 30% of what the final product will do. Show it anyway!

words.usageNote: Prototype = early, testable version. The goal is to learn fast and fail cheap. Build → test → improve → repeat.

words.simpleMeaning: combining separate systems or components into one

sentences.daily: The payment integration was completed in two days.
sentences.office: System integration was the most complex part of the project.
sentences.interview: Third-party integration allows the app to work with other tools.
sentences.business: The CRM integration pulled data from three separate systems.
sentences.formal: API-based integration ensured real-time data sync.
sentences.informal: Calendar integration: now my phone knows my meetings before I do.

words.usageNote: Integration = connecting two systems so they work as one. API integration is the most common type.

words.simpleMeaning: a visual display of key data and metrics

sentences.daily: The sales dashboard shows real-time revenue and leads.
sentences.office: I built a dashboard in Google Data Studio.
sentences.interview: All KPIs are tracked on the management dashboard.
sentences.business: The dashboard refreshes every 15 minutes automatically.
sentences.formal: The live dashboard impressed the investors at the demo.
sentences.informal: My life could use a dashboard: health, finances, productivity. Currently: chaos.

words.usageNote: Dashboard = one screen showing all important metrics. Good dashboards show what matters — nothing else.

words.simpleMeaning: a system that moves and processes data from source to destination

sentences.daily: The data pipeline ingests 10 million records per day.
sentences.office: A broken data pipeline means no reports for anyone.
sentences.interview: We built a real-time data pipeline for streaming analytics.
sentences.business: The pipeline moves data from the source system to the data warehouse.
sentences.formal: The data pipeline was automated using Apache Airflow.
sentences.informal: Data pipeline broke. Entire analytics team in crisis mode. It was a comma.

words.usageNote: Data pipeline = the automated journey of data from collection → processing → storage → analysis. Automate it!

words.simpleMeaning: the process of releasing software for users

sentences.daily: The deployment went live at midnight on Saturday.
sentences.office: Post-deployment monitoring caught two issues in 30 minutes.
sentences.interview: Automated deployment reduced release time from 2 days to 2 hours.
sentences.business: The staging environment mirrors production for safe deployment.
sentences.formal: Deployment to production requires approval from the QA lead.
sentences.informal: Deployment successful. App down in 3 minutes. Classic.

words.usageNote: Deployment = taking your code from 'done' to 'live for users.' Always test in staging before production deployment!

words.simpleMeaning: a system for tracking changes to code or files over time

sentences.daily: Version control lets teams work on code simultaneously.
sentences.office: Git is the most widely used version control system.
sentences.interview: Without version control, overwriting someone else's work is easy.
sentences.business: Version control allowed us to roll back after the bad deployment.
sentences.formal: Every developer should know version control before joining a team.
sentences.informal: Version control: because 'final_v2_FINAL_actualfinal.docx' is not a system.

words.usageNote: Version control (Git) = track every change to your code. Branch = work on a feature. Merge = combine it back.

words.simpleMeaning: an AI system modelled on the human brain

sentences.daily: Image recognition is powered by neural networks.
sentences.office: A neural network learns patterns from millions of examples.
sentences.interview: Deep learning uses multi-layer neural networks.
sentences.business: The neural network identified fraud with 99.2% accuracy.
sentences.formal: Neural networks require significant computational power to train.
sentences.informal: Neural network: trained on 10 billion words. Me: still learning 'whom' vs 'who'.

words.usageNote: Neural networks = layers of connected nodes (like brain neurons). They learn by seeing lots of examples.

words.simpleMeaning: technology applied to financial services

sentences.daily: Fintech companies like Paytm changed how India pays.
sentences.office: Fintech is disrupting traditional banking and insurance.
sentences.interview: I'm considering a career in fintech after my MBA.
sentences.business: Fintech startups raised a record $135 billion globally last year.
sentences.formal: The fintech platform enabled instant loans with AI-based credit scoring.
sentences.informal: Fintech: the reason I haven't been to a bank in 3 years.

words.usageNote: Fintech = Finance + Technology. Includes digital payments, online banking, robo-advisors, and crypto platforms.

words.simpleMeaning: technology applied to education

sentences.daily: This app is an example of edtech at its best.
sentences.office: Edtech platforms like BYJU'S and Coursera grew during the pandemic.
sentences.interview: Edtech is making quality education accessible worldwide.
sentences.business: The edtech company raised ₹500 crore to expand to rural India.
sentences.formal: AI-powered edtech personalises learning based on student progress.
sentences.informal: Edtech: because school at home during COVID taught us a lot about WiFi.

words.usageNote: Edtech = Education + Technology. Includes apps, platforms, LMS, and AI tutors. A fast-growing sector in India.

words.simpleMeaning: a network of physical devices connected to the internet

sentences.daily: IoT devices include smart thermostats, watches, and cars.
sentences.office: IoT helps factories monitor machines in real time.
sentences.interview: The smart home is powered entirely by IoT devices.
sentences.business: IoT security is a major concern as more devices connect.
sentences.formal: Our supply chain uses IoT sensors to track inventory.
sentences.informal: IoT: my fridge knows I'm out of milk. I didn't ask. It told me.

words.usageNote: IoT = everyday objects connected to the internet and sharing data. Examples: smart bulbs, fitness trackers, smart meters.

words.simpleMeaning: a contract defining the expected level of service

sentences.daily: Our SLA guarantees 99.9% uptime for all clients.
sentences.office: Breach of SLA results in service credits.
sentences.interview: Always negotiate SLA terms before signing with a vendor.
sentences.business: The cloud provider's SLA covers response time and resolution.
sentences.formal: SLA compliance is reviewed monthly by the operations team.
sentences.informal: SLA says 4-hour response. Actual response: 48 hours. SLA violated.

words.usageNote: SLA = a contract between service provider and client. It sets MINIMUM standards for availability, response, and quality.

words.simpleMeaning: the simplest version of a product that can be released

sentences.daily: Launch an MVP first — get feedback before building more.
sentences.office: Our MVP had just three features but solved the core problem.
sentences.interview: The startup's MVP attracted 10,000 users in its first week.
sentences.business: Investors funded us based on the MVP demo.
sentences.formal: Build the MVP in 4 weeks, not 4 months.
sentences.informal: My MVP lifestyle: minimum effort, viable results. Working on that.

words.usageNote: MVP = the SMALLEST product that still delivers value to the user. Launch it, learn from real users, then build more.

words.simpleMeaning: a plan showing a product's development priorities over time

sentences.daily: The product roadmap is reviewed every quarter.
sentences.office: Share the roadmap with stakeholders for alignment.
sentences.interview: The product roadmap prioritises features by impact.
sentences.business: Roadmap items are linked to business goals and user needs.
sentences.formal: The investor presentation included a 12-month product roadmap.
sentences.informal: My personal roadmap: English fluent by Day 75. On track!

words.usageNote: Product roadmap = visual plan of what you'll build and when. It answers: What? When? Why? for every feature.

words.simpleMeaning: the combination of technologies used to build a product

sentences.daily: Our tech stack is React, Node.js, and MongoDB.
sentences.office: Choose your tech stack based on scalability and team skills.
sentences.interview: A modern SaaS tech stack includes cloud, CI/CD, and microservices.
sentences.business: The tech stack was evaluated before building the new platform.
sentences.formal: Interview question: 'What is your preferred tech stack?'
sentences.informal: My current tech stack: Google, YouTube, and hope.

words.usageNote: Tech stack = all the tools and languages used together to build and run a product. Full-stack = front + back end.

words.simpleMeaning: an architecture where a system is split into small, independent services

sentences.daily: Netflix uses microservices to scale independently.
sentences.office: Microservices allow teams to deploy updates without affecting the whole system.
sentences.interview: We migrated from a monolith to microservices over 18 months.
sentences.business: Each microservice handles one business function — and one only.
sentences.formal: Microservices require strong orchestration and communication design.
sentences.informal: Microservices: when your app is 300 tiny apps wearing a trench coat.

words.usageNote: Microservices = small independent services that do ONE thing well. Easier to scale, update, and debug independently.

words.simpleMeaning: comparing two versions of something to see which performs better

sentences.daily: We A/B tested two email subject lines — B won by 30%.
sentences.office: A/B testing helps you make data-driven design decisions.
sentences.interview: Run A/B tests before rolling out any major change.
sentences.business: The A/B test showed Version B had 25% higher conversion.
sentences.formal: A/B testing removed gut feeling from product decisions.
sentences.informal: A/B testing my morning routine: alarm at 6 vs alarm at 7. 7 won.

words.usageNote: A/B testing = show Version A to Group A and Version B to Group B. Measure. Pick the winner. No guessing.

words.simpleMeaning: the percentage of users who take a desired action

sentences.daily: Our conversion rate improved from 2% to 5% after the redesign.
sentences.office: Conversion rate = buyers / total visitors × 100.
sentences.interview: Optimise the landing page to increase conversion rate.
sentences.business: A 1% increase in conversion rate doubled monthly revenue.
sentences.formal: The conversion rate was tracked in the CRM dashboard.
sentences.informal: Conversion rate for me completing side projects: approximately 0%.

words.usageNote: Conversion rate = % of visitors who DO what you want them to do (buy, sign up, download). Optimise this obsessively.

words.simpleMeaning: the rate at which customers stop using a product

sentences.daily: A high churn rate signals poor product-market fit.
sentences.office: We reduced churn rate by 15% through proactive customer success.
sentences.interview: Churn rate = customers lost / total customers × 100.
sentences.business: Even a 1% reduction in churn can add millions in revenue.
sentences.formal: The subscription product's churn rate was reviewed monthly.
sentences.informal: My gym has a high churn rate. I contributed significantly.

words.usageNote: Churn = losing customers. High churn = problem. Low churn = happy customers. Retention is the goal.

words.simpleMeaning: a short description of a feature from the user's perspective

sentences.daily: A user story follows this format: 'As a [user], I want to [action] so that [benefit].'
sentences.office: Each sprint is planned around a set of user stories.
sentences.interview: The product manager wrote 25 user stories for Q3.
sentences.business: User stories keep development focused on real user needs.
sentences.formal: The development team estimated effort for each user story.
sentences.informal: User story: As a person, I want chai so that I can function.

words.usageNote: User story = describes a feature from the USER's point of view. Keeps the team focused on solving real problems.

words.simpleMeaning: the part of a website or app the user sees and interacts with

sentences.daily: The frontend was built with React and Tailwind CSS.
sentences.office: A good frontend makes complex features feel simple.
sentences.interview: Frontend developers focus on performance, design, and usability.
sentences.business: The frontend loads in under 1 second on mobile.
sentences.formal: Frontend and backend must work seamlessly together.
sentences.informal: Frontend: the beautiful face. Backend: the brain. Both need to function.

words.usageNote: Frontend = what users see and click. HTML, CSS, JavaScript. React, Vue, Angular are popular frontend frameworks.

words.simpleMeaning: the server-side of an app that processes data

sentences.daily: The backend processes requests and returns responses.
sentences.office: The backend was built with Node.js and PostgreSQL.
sentences.interview: Backend bugs are harder to find but more critical to fix.
sentences.business: The payment backend handled 10,000 transactions per minute.
sentences.formal: A robust backend ensures reliability, security, and scalability.
sentences.informal: Backend error. Please contact support. (Support: also me.)

words.usageNote: Backend = where data is stored, processed, and served. Python, Java, Go, Node.js are popular backend languages.

words.simpleMeaning: all the code that makes up a software project

sentences.daily: New developers need time to understand the codebase.
sentences.office: A well-documented codebase saves hours of onboarding time.
sentences.interview: Technical debt accumulates when the codebase isn't cleaned up.
sentences.business: The codebase was refactored before the major feature release.
sentences.formal: Our codebase has 500,000 lines — and counting.
sentences.informal: Inheriting an undocumented codebase: a rite of passage.

words.usageNote: Codebase = all the source code for a project. Clean codebases are easier to maintain, test, and scale.

words.simpleMeaning: code that was done quickly and needs to be improved later

sentences.daily: We carry tech debt from our rapid MVP phase.
sentences.office: Ignoring tech debt slows future development significantly.
sentences.interview: Every sprint, we allocate 20% of time to paying down tech debt.
sentences.business: Tech debt compounds — fix it early, not late.
sentences.formal: The codebase had massive tech debt from 3 years of shortcuts.
sentences.informal: Tech debt: future me's problem. (Future me is angry about this.)

words.usageNote: Tech debt = shortcuts taken now that create problems later. Like borrowing time from the future. Always costs more.

words.simpleMeaning: the time a system is fully operational and available

sentences.daily: We guarantee 99.9% uptime — that's less than 9 hours of downtime per year.
sentences.office: High uptime is critical for e-commerce platforms.
sentences.interview: The server's uptime was monitored 24/7 by the ops team.
sentences.business: Downtime during peak hours cost the company ₹50 lakh.
sentences.formal: Uptime improved after migrating to a redundant cloud architecture.
sentences.informal: My brain's uptime: 7 AM–1 PM. After that, please email.

words.usageNote: 99.9% uptime = 8.77 hours downtime/year. 99.99% = 52.6 minutes. Every 9 matters. Reliability is a premium feature.

words.simpleMeaning: to restructure existing code without changing its behaviour

sentences.daily: Refactor the code before adding new features.
sentences.office: We refactored the payment module to reduce complexity.
sentences.interview: Regular refactoring prevents tech debt from accumulating.
sentences.business: The refactored code was 40% shorter and easier to maintain.
sentences.formal: The team spent a full sprint refactoring the legacy codebase.
sentences.informal: Refactored the code. Same functionality. 30% fewer headaches.

words.simpleMeaning: distributing workload across multiple servers

sentences.daily: Load balancing prevents any single server from crashing.
sentences.office: We implemented load balancing before the product launch.
sentences.interview: Cloud providers offer automatic load balancing services.
sentences.business: Without load balancing, the server crashed during a traffic spike.
sentences.formal: Load balancing ensured zero downtime during the campaign.
sentences.informal: Load balancing: like a fair manager who shares work equally.

words.usageNote: Load balancing = distribute incoming requests across multiple servers so no one server gets overwhelmed. Essential at scale.

words.simpleMeaning: storing frequently accessed data temporarily for faster access

sentences.daily: Caching reduced our API response time by 90%.
sentences.office: Clear your browser cache if the page isn't updating.
sentences.interview: We cache homepage data to handle 100,000 users per second.
sentences.business: Redis is a popular in-memory caching tool.
sentences.formal: Caching cuts database load by storing repeated query results.
sentences.informal: Caching: teaching your computer to remember things so you don't repeat yourself.

words.usageNote: Caching = store data temporarily for fast retrieval. Without it, every request hits the database — slow and expensive.

words.simpleMeaning: an automated message sent from one app to another when an event occurs

sentences.daily: The payment webhook notifies our system when a transaction completes.
sentences.office: Webhooks eliminate the need to repeatedly poll for updates.
sentences.interview: We set up a webhook to trigger an email when an order ships.
sentences.business: The developer configured 10 webhooks for different user events.
sentences.formal: Webhook delivery failure was detected and retried automatically.
sentences.informal: Webhook fired. Our system got notified. No one had to refresh the page. Magic.

words.usageNote: Webhook = a push notification from one system to another. Triggered by an event. More efficient than polling.

words.simpleMeaning: a software system built as a single, large unit

sentences.daily: Our app started as a monolith and grew unmanageable.
sentences.office: Many startups begin with a monolith — it's faster to build.
sentences.interview: Moving from a monolith to microservices takes years.
sentences.business: A well-structured monolith is better than badly designed microservices.
sentences.formal: The monolith handled 1 million users before we needed to split it.
sentences.informal: Monolith: one giant system. Works great until it doesn't.

words.usageNote: Monolith = everything in one place. Faster to build. Harder to scale and maintain at large scale.

words.simpleMeaning: the foundational systems supporting a technology product

sentences.daily: Cloud infrastructure scales automatically with demand.
sentences.office: Investing in infrastructure early prevents outages later.
sentences.interview: The team manages infrastructure on AWS.
sentences.business: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) automates server configuration.
sentences.formal: The infrastructure team rebuilt the entire system in 6 months.
sentences.informal: Good infrastructure is invisible. Bad infrastructure is all you can see.

words.usageNote: Infrastructure = the underlying systems (servers, networks, databases) that everything runs on. Without it: nothing works.

words.simpleMeaning: a team meeting after a sprint to review what worked and what didn't

sentences.daily: Our retrospective always ends with three action items.
sentences.office: Retrospectives are not blame sessions — they're improvement tools.
sentences.interview: We identify what to start, stop, and continue in each retrospective.
sentences.business: The retrospective revealed that daily standups were too long.
sentences.formal: Every sprint ends with a retrospective to learn and improve.
sentences.informal: Retrospective insight: we talk too much. Noted. (Took 90 minutes to note this.)

words.usageNote: Sprint retrospective = look back at the last sprint. What went well? What didn't? What will you change? Honest and safe.

words.simpleMeaning: a prioritised list of features or tasks to be built

sentences.daily: The product backlog had 120 items at the start of Q1.
sentences.office: Prioritise the backlog based on user impact and effort.
sentences.interview: Only the Product Owner can add items to the product backlog.
sentences.business: The backlog is reviewed and refined every two weeks.
sentences.formal: Backlog grooming keeps the list clean and actionable.
sentences.informal: Backlog: 300 items. Sprint capacity: 10. Maths is brutal.

words.usageNote: Product backlog = everything the team WANTS to build. Sprint backlog = what they WILL build this sprint. Big difference.

words.simpleMeaning: happening or processing instantly without delay

sentences.daily: The dashboard shows real-time sales data.
sentences.office: Real-time notifications improve user engagement dramatically.
sentences.interview: We built a real-time chat feature using WebSockets.
sentences.business: Real-time fraud detection prevents losses instantly.
sentences.formal: The system processes real-time data from 10,000 IoT sensors.
sentences.informal: Real-time update: still procrastinating. Thanks for the notification.

words.usageNote: Real-time = no delay between event and response. Essential for trading, gaming, chat, and IoT applications.

words.simpleMeaning: integrating security practices into DevOps processes

sentences.daily: DevSecOps embeds security at every stage of development.
sentences.office: We shifted from DevOps to DevSecOps after a data breach.
sentences.interview: Security scanning is automated in our DevSecOps pipeline.
sentences.business: DevSecOps ensures vulnerabilities are caught before deployment.
sentences.formal: The team adopted DevSecOps to reduce compliance risk.
sentences.informal: DevSecOps: because security can't be an afterthought anymore.

words.usageNote: DevSecOps = Development + Security + Operations. Security is built IN, not bolted ON. Caught early = cheaper to fix.

words.simpleMeaning: an AI model trained on vast text to generate and understand language

sentences.daily: ChatGPT is an LLM that generates text based on prompts.
sentences.office: LLMs are trained on billions of words from the internet.
sentences.interview: The company built a custom LLM for healthcare queries.
sentences.business: LLMs can summarise, translate, code, and explain.
sentences.formal: LLMs have fundamentally changed how we write, search, and code.
sentences.informal: LLM: who I ask when Google doesn't understand me either.

words.usageNote: LLM = AI trained on huge amounts of text to predict and generate language. GPT-4, Claude, Gemini are examples.

words.simpleMeaning: the skill of crafting effective inputs for AI models

sentences.daily: Good prompt engineering gets you 10× better AI results.
sentences.office: Prompt engineering is a new skill every knowledge worker needs.
sentences.interview: I prompt engineered my way to a perfect essay outline.
sentences.business: The researcher specialised in prompt engineering for LLMs.
sentences.formal: Prompt engineering involves structure, context, and clear instructions.
sentences.informal: Prompt engineering: asking the AI politely and specifically. Works for humans too.

words.usageNote: Prompt engineering = designing clear, specific instructions for AI. Better prompts = better outputs. 'Garbage in, garbage out.'
